summary: RBC downgrades AB Foods to underperform citing Primark growth concerns and valuation risks
body: Time Period Forward-looking (RBC forecast changes for FY2026–FY2028; expectation around ABF H1 results) RBC Capital Markets downgrades AB Foods to underperform (from sector perform). RBC cuts its price target to 1,850p (from 2,050p) (-9.8%). RBC sees further downside risk to consensus earnings, mainly driven by pressure on Primark. RBC quote (Primark / valuation): "Although we think a demerger should make ABF more investable in the long run, we think ABF's valuation is full given more limited growth prospects over the next few years," (Richard Chamberlain). RBC concern: Primark’s "value for money perception" could be damaged by attempts to elevate its offering. RBC highlights Primark’s relatively low gross margin, increasing sensitivity to rising cost pressures. RBC expects mixed performance in the Food arm this year. RBC notes Twinings has been "performing well". RBC notes Ovaltine has delivered "good results from marketing investments". RBC flags US pressure in cooking oils and bakery ingredients after a period of elevated pricing. RBC expects ABF to confirm plans to demerge Primark and Food at its first half results. RBC quote (growth outlook pre-demerger): "However, in advance of the demerger we think the growth outlook for both sides of the business looks challenging". RBC lowers its pretax profit forecasts for FY2026, FY2027 and FY2028 by 4%–7%. RBC says its revised forecasts are now 2%–10% below consensus. RBC lists upside risks including FX, a Sugar unit recovery, and further value-accretive M&A.

summary: Grove Court portfolio performing well with 97% occupancy and 5% rent increase secured
body: Time Period Post-acquisition update (Grove Court acquired in February; current occupancy / rent review outcome) The recently acquired Grove Court portfolio has performed in line with expectations. Portfolio occupancy remains ~97%. A rent review at a motor dealership in Beaconsfield (Grove Court’s largest asset by income) delivered a 5% increase in passing rent. Custodian acquired the family-owned Grove Court portfolio in February for £35.9m. Richard Shepherd-Cross quote (portfolio rationale): "Following the strategic majority-share acquisition of Grove Court earlier this year, its complimentary properties have improved our existing portfolio by providing tenant diversification and further defensive income to help drive earnings, while helping us continue to add scale to our business," Richard Shepherd-Cross quote (uplift / latent value): "We have already secured a 5% rental uplift with the occupier at Grove Court’s largest asset by income, further cementing our belief that we can capture the latent value in this portfolio."

summary: UK government contracts Rolls-Royce to build three small modular nuclear reactors
body: Time Period Programme launch / next-stage development (contract signed; work starts immediately; milestones through to final investment decision) Government-owned GBE-N signed a “landmark contract” with Rolls-Royce SMR, enabling work to start immediately on three small modular reactors (SMRs). The UK government says the three SMRs should generate enough low-carbon electricity to power the equivalent of ~3 million homes. Alongside the contract, the National Wealth Fund plans to invest up to £599m to support Rolls-Royce SMR through next-stage design. Ministers describe this as the first formal contract for delivering SMRs in Europe. The project is expected to support ~3,000 jobs at peak construction, plus thousands more across the UK supply chain. Rolls-Royce SMR was selected as the preferred partner in June last year after a competitive process. The government allocated £2.6bn for the SMR programme. The government has said the first SMR nuclear power station will be built at Wylfa (North Wales) by GBE-N. Chancellor Rachel Reeves quote: "will strengthen our energy security, create skilled jobs and help to build a new generation of homegrown nuclear technology that will power our economy for decades to come". Rachel Reeves quote: "We have the right economic plan – one where growth and clean energy go hand-in-hand – and one that will benefit everyone across the country," The contract requires delivery against key milestones, starting with site-specific design, regulatory engagement, and planning processes, before a final decision on capital investment to proceed with construction. Energy Secretary Ed Miliband quote: "At a time of global instability, this is a major milestone for Britain's energy security." Ed Miliband quote: "We are backing a British company to deliver our first small modular reactors – creating a generation of good jobs, driving growth and providing clean, homegrown power for decades to come." Ed Miliband quote: "Our clean energy mission is the only route to getting off the rollercoaster of fossil fuels and take back control of our energy independence." GBE-N CEO Simon Roddy quote: "This agreement is a landmark moment for the nuclear industry." Simon Roddy quote: "Supporting skills, innovation and growing our industrial capability is essential to this partnership, and will ensure the UK is well-placed to deliver the next generation of nuclear infrastructure." GBE-N says it has already awarded over £350m of supply-chain contracts this year. National Wealth Fund CEO Oliver Holbourn quote: "significant moment for the future of our nuclear industry". Oliver Holbourn quote: "The development and deployment of Rolls-Royce SMR's technology here in the UK will create thousands of jobs and pave the way for an affordable, cleaner, and more secure energy system," Rolls-Royce SMR is the preferred SMR technology partner for Czech utility CEZ, with plans to provide up to 3GW of low-carbon energy in Czechia. Rolls-Royce SMR CEO Chris Cholerton quote: "This contract unlocks the delivery of our first three units at Wylfa and brings certainty to the UK SMR programme." Chris Cholerton quote: "With plans for up to six further units in Czechia, this announcement makes Rolls-Royce SMR the only company with multiple commitments in Europe."

summary: RBC downgrades WH Smith to sector perform, cuts price target to 650p citing UK and Rest of World headwinds
body: Time Period Forward-looking (RBC forecast changes for FY2026–FY2027; commentary on current trading drivers) RBC downgrades WH Smith to sector perform (from outperform). RBC cuts its price target to 650p (from 675p) (-3.7%). RBC lowers FY2026 and FY2027 pretax profit forecasts by 3%–4%, citing a more cautious view on the UK and Rest of World outlook (aligned to passenger-number expectations). RBC says its revised estimates now "sit just below" WH Smith’s FY2026 guidance. RBC expects UK pressure from a weaker employment outlook and higher air fares post Covid. RBC notes disruption from store closures at Heathrow Terminals 3–5. Separately, WH Smith confirms it is opening three new flagship stores at those Heathrow terminals. In the US, RBC expects Travel Essentials to continue performing well, supported by new store openings and higher passenger spend. RBC expects the group’s Rest of World focus to be on Australia, Ireland and Spain. RBC expects the profitability impact from the Middle East war to be "relatively immaterial". RBC quote: "Geopolitical risk aside, we think WH Smith still offers potential for an attractive longer term travel growth plus cash returns equity story, given its category mix management and operational/logistics skills, in what are captive markets," RBC adds WH Smith does not have surplus capital to return currently and is likely to prioritise balance sheet strengthening.

summary: Panmure Liberum downgrades Wickes to hold and cuts FY2026-27 profit forecasts by 7% and 4%
body: Time Period Forward-looking (Panmure Liberum view on consensus; forecast changes for FY2026–FY2027) Panmure Liberum downgrades Wickes to hold (from buy). Panmure Liberum cuts its price target to 215p (from 230p) (-6.5%). Panmure Liberum says consensus implies ~35% cumulative pretax profit growth to FY2027, which it views as too ambitious. Panmure Liberum quote: "Even under supportive assumptions, this would require significant annual productivity savings, which appears increasingly demanding given the efficiencies already delivered since FY22," Panmure Liberum sees upside pockets in Design & Installation and longer-term store rollout, but flags near-term execution risk. Panmure Liberum says TradePro growth appears to be maturing. Panmure Liberum says faster rollout of smaller-footprint stores is likely earnings dilutive and cash consumptive near term. Panmure Liberum cuts pretax profit forecasts by 7% for FY2026 and ~4% for FY2027. Panmure Liberum says this implies 18% cumulative pretax profit growth to FY2027 and puts it at the lower end of forecasts. Panmure Liberum quote: "In the absence of upgrades, and given downside risks we have identified, we downgrade our recommendation to hold,"

summary: LGI Homes launches Waters Farm community in Woodruff, South Carolina with homes starting in the $270s
body: Time Period Community launch / sales offer (pricing “starting in the $270s”; full build-out plan) LGI Homes announces Waters Farm, a new single-family community in Woodruff, South Carolina. Location positioning: short drive to Greenville and Spartanburg; commute access to employers including BMW Manufacturing, GE, and Michelin. Homes: three- and four-bedroom single-family across six floor plans, with four designs new to the Greenville market. Included “CompleteHome™” upgrades at no additional cost: energy-efficient Whirlpool® appliances, granite countertops, luxury vinyl plank flooring, recessed lighting, landscaped front yards. Other included features: two-car garages, open-concept layouts, builder-paid closing costs. Quote (Chris Cositore): “Waters Farm offers the perfect balance of community, comfort and accessibility,” Quote (Chris Cositore): “Homebuyers will enjoy the charm of downtown Woodruff paired with quick access to Greenville and Spartanburg, along with professionally designed homes that are ready to live in from day one.” Floor plans (sq ft): Arcadia 1,309; Harris 1,415; Carolina 1,700; Ashwood 2,060; Piedmont 1,886; Cherokee 2,320. Amenities: onsite children’s playground; proximity to McKinney Park, Woodruff Greenway, Croft State Park. Full build-out: 198 homesites. Pricing: starting in the $270s. Sales center: 112 Whiteheath Court, Woodruff, SC 29388; open daily; phone (877) 691-8369.

summary: BP expects exceptional Q1 2025 trading results driven by higher oil prices and refining margins
body:
Time Period Q1 2025 (three months to March 2025)

  • Expects an “exceptional” oil trading result in Q1 2025 after a “weak” Q4.
  • Brent crude average: USD81.13/bbl vs USD63.73/bbl in Q4 (+27%).
  • Refining indicator margin: USD16.9/bbl vs USD15.2/bbl (+11%).
  • Flagged “heightened volatility” in crude, gas and refined products due to the Middle East crisis, expected to impact results (including trading results and working capital movements).
  • Upstream production expected broadly flat vs Q4 (2,344 mboe/d); gas & low carbon slightly higher; oil slightly lower vs 1,555 mboe/d.
  • Gas & low carbon: realisations expected broadly flat; gas marketing & trading result expected to be “average”.
  • Customers & products: seasonally lower volumes and lower retail fuels margins, more than offset by stronger midstream performance and stronger realised refining margins.
  • Net debt expected USD25–27bn vs USD22.2bn at end-Q4 (+13% to +22%).
  • Net debt increase driven primarily by working capital build of USD4–7bn, “largely due to the price environment.”
  • Capex expected broadly flat vs USD3.5bn in the prior quarter.

company: Schroder European Real Estate Investment Trust Ltd
summary: Schroder European Real Estate portfolio valued at EUR192.6m, down 0.7% from year-end 2024
body:
Time Period Quarter ended 31 March 2025

  • Portfolio independently valued at EUR192.6m vs EUR194.0m at 31 Dec (-0.7% QoQ).
  • Valuation at 31 March 2025 also cited as EUR194.0m (implies -0.7% vs that comparator).
  • Rumilly and Stuttgart delivered strong valuation growth attributed to lease extensions.
  • Tenant-related setbacks impacted other assets, particularly Alkmaar and Cannes.

summary: Mortgage Advice Bureau acquires HomeOwners Alliance for £1.4m to expand customer reach
body:
Time Period Acquisition announcement (no reporting period stated)

  • Acquired HomeOwners Alliance Ltd (HOA) to engage customers earlier in the home-buying process.
  • Total consideration GBP1.4m, plus distributable reserves.
  • Payment terms: 50% on completion; remaining 50% payable over two years.
  • Acquired the full share capital from the founders and Smoove Ltd.
  • Rationale: strengthens positioning ahead of potential changes from the government’s ongoing home-selling and buying consultation.
  • Expects integration/enhancement of HOA’s digital platform to increase lead volumes and extend the range of services.
  • Expects to strengthen connections between mortgage advice and complementary home-moving services.
  • Ben Thompson (Director of Home Moving Strategy) said:

“HomeOwners Alliance has built a strong reputation as a trusted source of information for homeowners and prospective buyers,” “This acquisition strengthens our ability to engage with customers earlier in their homebuying journey and supports our strategy to broaden MAB's role across the wider home-moving process. We're pleased to welcome HomeOwners Alliance to the Group and look forward to supporting its continued growth.”

summary: Morgan Stanley downgrades UK housebuilders citing macro risks from Middle East conflict and rate pressures
body:
Time Period Analyst note (macro/rates outlook)

  • Warned UK housebuilders face rising “macro risk” as the Middle East conflict “alters the industry backdrop materially.”
  • Transmission mechanism cited: higher oil/energy prices → higher swap rates → reduced expectations for Bank of England cuts → weaker housing demand.
  • Analysts said: “Valuation support alone has repeatedly failed to act as a catalyst in this cycle.”
  • Downgraded Barratt Redrow to equal-weight from overweight; cut price target to 300p from 430p (-30%).
  • Downgraded Berkeley Group to underweight from equal-weight; cut price target to 2,990p from 3,840p (-22%).
  • Price target cuts: Persimmon 1,390p from 1,610p (-14%); Taylor Wimpey 80p from 90p (-11%); Vistry 380p from 510p (-26%).
  • Ratings: Persimmon overweight (housebuilding preference), Vistry equal-weight, Taylor Wimpey underweight.
  • On Berkeley: cited “cut profit forecasts, paused land acquisition and prioritised balance sheet strength over growth amid weak London and South East demand,” and referenced re-phasing ‘Berkeley 2035’ delivery over four years to 2030.
  • On Barratt Redrow: “scale and a multi-brand platform offer strategic flexibility” but revenue synergies constrained in weak demand; performance increasingly macro-driven.
  • On Persimmon: viewed as “more appealing” and “the cleanest way to gain exposure to a volume-led recovery, should rate conditions ease,” citing above-average margins/returns and limited exposure to London.

summary: Taseko Mines reports strong Q1 production growth with Florence Copper ramp-up and Gibraltar output surge
body:
Time Period First quarter production update (comparatives provided vs Q1 2025)

  • Florence Copper SX/EW plant commenced operations mid-February; first copper cathodes harvested end-February.
  • Florence Copper Q1 production: 1.5m lbs copper cathode.
  • Solutions flowing in the wellfield since late 2025; initial leaching/production in line with expectations based on modelling.
  • Operational focus: balancing solution flow and grades from the wellfield through to cathode production.
  • CEO Stuart McDonald said:

“We are happy with the results from the first months of in-situ copper production at Florence. With the successful start-up behind us, the focus is turning to the production ramp-up. Additional newly constructed wells are now being integrated into the system, which will allow for higher solution flows and copper production in the coming weeks. Wellfield expansion continues, with four drills currently operating and a fifth expected to commence shortly.”

  • Gibraltar Q1 production: 30.0m lbs copper and 717k lbs molybdenum.
  • YoY vs Q1 2025: copper +50%; molybdenum +113%.
  • Recoveries improved to 83%; copper grades in line with life-of-mine average.
  • Copper sales: 27m lbs, slightly below production due to shipment timing.
  • Gibraltar SX/EW plant produced 733k lbs copper cathode and operated continuously through winter.
  • Average LME copper price in Q1 was +16% vs previous quarter.
  • Diesel price increases could raise Gibraltar operating costs by US$0.10–US$0.15/lb this year.
  • Florence has a fixed price contract for all sulphuric acid requirements in 2026; expects no impact from recent inflation/supply chain issues.
  • Outlook statement: continues to anticipate strong financial performance from steady Gibraltar production and growing Florence production.

summary: NVIDIA releases Ising, open source quantum AI models for faster calibration and error correction
body:
Time Period Product announcement (no reporting period stated)

  • Announced NVIDIA Ising, described as “the world’s first family of open source quantum AI models” for quantum processor calibration and quantum error correction.
  • Claims: up to 2.5x faster performance and 3x higher accuracy for QEC decoding vs pyMatching.
  • Jensen Huang said: “AI is essential to making quantum computing practical… With Ising, AI becomes the control plane — the operating system of quantum machines — transforming fragile qubits to scalable and reliable quantum-GPU systems.”
  • Ising Calibration: vision language model to interpret/react to measurements; aims to reduce calibration time from days to hours.
  • Ising Decoding: two variants of a 3D CNN optimised for speed or accuracy for real-time QEC decoding.
  • Adoption claims: Calibration already in use by multiple enterprises and labs (incl. UK National Physical Laboratory); Decoding deployed by multiple universities and labs.
  • Includes workflow “cookbook,” training data, and NVIDIA NIM™ microservices; models can run locally.
  • Complements CUDA-Q™ and integrates with NVQLink™ for real-time control and error correction.
  • Market sizing cited: quantum computing market expected to surpass $11bn in 2030 (Resonance).

“We are happy with the results from the first months of in-situ copper production at Florence. With the successful start-up behind us, the focus is turning to the production ramp-up. Additional newly constructed wells are now being integrated into the system, which will allow for higher solution flows and copper production in the coming weeks. Wellfield expansion continues, with four drills currently operating and a fifth expected to commence shortly.”

  • Gibraltar Q1 production: 30.0m lbs copper and 717k lbs molybdenum.
  • YoY vs Q1 2025: copper +50%; molybdenum +113%.
  • Recoveries improved to 83%; copper grades in line with life-of-mine average.
  • Copper sales: 27m lbs, slightly below production due to shipment timing.
  • Gibraltar SX/EW plant produced 733k lbs copper cathode and operated continuously through winter.
  • Average LME copper price in Q1 was +16% vs previous quarter.
  • Diesel price increases could raise Gibraltar operating costs by US$0.10–US$0.15/lb this year.
  • Florence has a fixed price contract for all sulphuric acid requirements in 2026; expects no impact from recent inflation/supply chain issues.
  • Outlook statement: continues to anticipate strong financial performance from steady Gibraltar production and growing Florence production.

summary: NVIDIA launches Ising, open source quantum AI models for processor calibration and error correction
body:
Time Period Product announcement (no reporting period stated)

  • Announced NVIDIA Ising, described as “the world’s first family of open source quantum AI models” for quantum processor calibration and quantum error correction.
  • Claims: up to 2.5x faster performance and 3x higher accuracy for QEC decoding vs pyMatching.
  • Jensen Huang said: “AI is essential to making quantum computing practical… With Ising, AI becomes the control plane — the operating system of quantum machines — transforming fragile qubits to scalable and reliable quantum-GPU systems.”
  • Ising Calibration: vision language model to interpret/react to measurements; aims to reduce calibration time from days to hours.
  • Ising Decoding: two variants of a 3D CNN optimised for speed or accuracy for real-time QEC decoding.
  • Adoption claims: Calibration already in use by multiple enterprises and labs (incl. UK National Physical Laboratory); Decoding deployed by multiple universities and labs.
  • Includes workflow “cookbook,” training data, and NVIDIA NIM™ microservices; models can run locally.
  • Complements CUDA-Q™ and integrates with NVQLink™ for real-time control and error correction.
  • Market sizing cited: quantum computing market expected to surpass $11bn in 2030 (Resonance).

summary: BP expects exceptional Q1 2025 trading results from higher oil prices and refining margins
body:
Time Period Q1 2025 (three months to March 2025)

  • Expects an “exceptional” oil trading result in Q1 2025 after a “weak” Q4.
  • Brent crude average: USD81.13/bbl vs USD63.73/bbl in Q4 (+27%).
  • Refining indicator margin: USD16.9/bbl vs USD15.2/bbl (+11%).
  • Flagged “heightened volatility” in crude, gas and refined products due to the Middle East crisis, expected to impact results (including trading results and working capital movements).
  • Upstream production expected broadly flat vs Q4 (2,344 mboe/d); gas & low carbon slightly higher; oil slightly lower vs 1,555 mboe/d.
  • Gas & low carbon: realisations expected broadly flat; gas marketing & trading result expected to be “average”.
  • Customers & products: seasonally lower volumes and lower retail fuels margins, more than offset by stronger midstream performance and stronger realised refining margins.
  • Net debt expected USD25–27bn vs USD22.2bn at end-Q4 (+13% to +22%).
  • Net debt increase driven primarily by working capital build of USD4–7bn, “largely due to the price environment.”
  • Capex expected broadly flat vs USD3.5bn in the prior quarter.

company: Schroder European Real Estate Investment Trust Ltd
summary: European real estate portfolio declined 0.7% to EUR192.6m despite strong Rumilly and Stuttgart performance
body:
Time Period Quarter ended 31 March 2025

  • Portfolio independently valued at EUR192.6m vs EUR194.0m at 31 Dec (-0.7% QoQ).
  • Valuation at 31 March 2025 also cited as EUR194.0m (implies -0.7% vs that comparator).
  • Rumilly and Stuttgart delivered strong valuation growth attributed to lease extensions.
  • Tenant-related setbacks impacted other assets, particularly Alkmaar and Cannes.

summary: MAB acquires HomeOwners Alliance for £1.4m to expand early customer engagement in home-buying
body:
Time Period Acquisition announcement (no reporting period stated)

  • Acquired HomeOwners Alliance Ltd (HOA) to engage customers earlier in the home-buying process.
  • Total consideration GBP1.4m, plus distributable reserves.
  • Payment terms: 50% on completion; remaining 50% payable over two years.
  • Acquired the full share capital from the founders and Smoove Ltd.
  • Rationale: strengthens positioning ahead of potential changes from the government’s ongoing home-selling and buying consultation.
  • Expects integration/enhancement of HOA’s digital platform to increase lead volumes and extend the range of services.
  • Expects to strengthen connections between mortgage advice and complementary home-moving services.
  • Ben Thompson (Director of Home Moving Strategy) said:

“HomeOwners Alliance has built a strong reputation as a trusted source of information for homeowners and prospective buyers,” “This acquisition strengthens our ability to engage with customers earlier in their homebuying journey and supports our strategy to broaden MAB's role across the wider home-moving process. We're pleased to welcome HomeOwners Alliance to the Group and look forward to supporting its continued growth.”

summary: Morgan Stanley downgrades UK housebuilders citing macro risks from Middle East conflict and rate pressures
body:
Time Period Analyst note (macro/rates outlook)

  • Warned UK housebuilders face rising “macro risk” as the Middle East conflict “alters the industry backdrop materially.”
  • Transmission mechanism cited: higher oil/energy prices → higher swap rates → reduced expectations for Bank of England cuts → weaker housing demand.
  • Analysts said: “Valuation support alone has repeatedly failed to act as a catalyst in this cycle.”
  • Downgraded Barratt Redrow to equal-weight from overweight; cut price target to 300p from 430p (-30%).
  • Downgraded Berkeley Group to underweight from equal-weight; cut price target to 2,990p from 3,840p (-22%).
  • Price target cuts: Persimmon 1,390p from 1,610p (-14%); Taylor Wimpey 80p from 90p (-11%); Vistry 380p from 510p (-26%).
  • Ratings: Persimmon overweight (housebuilding preference), Vistry equal-weight, Taylor Wimpey underweight.
  • On Berkeley: cited “cut profit forecasts, paused land acquisition and prioritised balance sheet strength over growth amid weak London and South East demand,” and referenced re-phasing ‘Berkeley 2035’ delivery over four years to 2030.
  • On Barratt Redrow: “scale and a multi-brand platform offer strategic flexibility” but revenue synergies constrained in weak demand; performance increasingly macro-driven.
  • On Persimmon: viewed as “more appealing” and “the cleanest way to gain exposure to a volume-led recovery, should rate conditions ease,” citing above-average margins/returns and limited exposure to London.

summary: Taseko's Florence copper mine starts production while Gibraltar delivers strong year-over-year growth
body:

  • Florence Copper SX/EW plant commenced operations mid-February; first copper cathodes harvested end-February.

  • Solutions flowing in the wellfield since late 2025; initial leaching/production in line with expectations based on modelling.

Additional newly constructed wells are now being integrated into the system, which will allow for higher solution flows and copper production in the coming weeks. Wellfield expansion continues, with four drills currently operating and a fifth expected to commence shortly.”

  • Gibraltar Q1 production: 30.0m lbs copper and 717k lbs molybdenum.
  • YoY vs Q1 2025: copper +50%; molybdenum +113%.
  • Recoveries improved to 83%; copper grades in line with life-of-mine average.
  • Copper sales: 27m lbs, slightly below production due to shipment timing.
  • Gibraltar SX/EW plant produced 733k lbs copper cathode and operated continuously through winter.
  • Average LME copper price in Q1 was +16% vs previous quarter.
  • Diesel price increases could raise Gibraltar operating costs by US$0.10–US$0.15/lb this year.
  • Florence has a fixed price contract for all sulphuric acid requirements in 2026; expects no impact from recent inflation/supply chain issues.
  • Outlook statement: continues to anticipate strong financial performance from steady Gibraltar production and growing Florence production.

summary: Thérèse Esperdy appointed Chair of Rentokil Initial, commencing September 2026
body:
Time Period N/A (Board succession / appointments effective July–September 2026)

  • Appointment: Thérèse Esperdy appointed Chair of the Board, commencing 1 September 2026.
  • Board entry: Esperdy will join as Non-Executive Director and Chair-designate on 1 July 2026.
  • Succession context: Follows 5 March 2026 announcement that current Chair Richard Solomons (Chair since 2019) intended to retire.
  • Current external roles (at announcement): Non-Executive Chair of Imperial Brands plc (to retire following its November 2026 Board meeting and FY2026 results announcement), Senior Independent Director designate at Smith & Nephew plc, and Non-Executive Director at Moody’s Corporation.
  • Prior non-exec role: Previously Senior Independent Director of National Grid plc.
  • Executive background: Senior leadership roles at JP Morgan including Global Chair of Financial Institutions Group, Co-head of Asia-Pacific Corporate & Investment Banking, and Global Head of Debt Capital Markets.
  • Governance: Board determined Esperdy will be independent on appointment under the UK Corporate Governance Code.
  • Committee role: Esperdy to succeed Solomons as Chair of the Nomination Committee on 1 September 2026.
  • Remuneration: Esperdy will receive total fees of £725,000 per annum.
  • Outgoing Chair departure date: Richard Solomons will retire from the Board effective midnight on 31 August 2026.
  • Listing disclosures: Company states no additional details to disclose under Listing Rule 6.4.8 in respect of the appointment.
  • Quote (Senior Independent Director, John Pettigrew):

"We are delighted to welcome Thérèse to Rentokil Initial. She brings considerable experience to the Board from her wide-ranging non-executive roles in international businesses based in both the UK and the US. The Board is confident that she is the best possible candidate to lead the Board through the exciting next phase of Rentokil Initial's growth, working with Mike and the executive team. "On behalf of the Board, I would like to thank Richard for his strong, committed leadership and his significant contributions to the Company over many years."

  • Quote (Thérèse Esperdy, incoming Chair):

"It's a privilege to be appointed as Chair of the Board, and I am looking forward to working with my new colleagues to drive the Company forward and realise the great potential it has as a leading global provider of pest control and hygiene services."

  • Quote (Richard Solomons, outgoing Chair):

"It has been an immense privilege to serve as Chair of Rentokil Initial over what has been a period of growth and transformation for the business. With the successful appointment of Mike Duffy as CEO, and a clear strategy developed for North American growth, 2026 is the right time for me to step down and ensure a smooth handover to Thérèse. I have great confidence in the future success of Rentokil Initial - it's a high-quality business, full of exceptional people, operating in highly resilient growth markets."

summary: Barratt Redrow delivered solid Q3 with stronger reservation rates and accelerated synergy outlet rollout
body:
Time Period 13 weeks from 29 December 2025 to 29 March 2026 (Q3 FY26 trading update)

  • CEO framing: “solid third quarter” with “resilient reservation rate” and “good customer demand”.
  • Macro view: expects Middle East conflict to have limited impact on FY26 performance due to “strong forward sales position and advanced build programme”.
  • Guidance: still on track to deliver total housing completions and adjusted PBT in line with consensus.
  • Consensus (compiled 14 April 2026): FY26 adjusted PBT £568m (range £534m–£586m).
  • Consensus (compiled 14 April 2026): FY26 completions (incl. JVs) 17,416 (range 16,998–17,589).
  • Net private reservation rate (ex PRS/MUS): 0.64 vs 0.62 (+3.2%).
  • Net private reservation rate (incl. PRS/MUS): 0.67 vs 0.63 (+6.3%).
  • PRS/MUS contribution to reservation rate: 0.03 vs 0.01 (+200%, i.e., +0.02 absolute).
  • Sales incentives: “in line with HY26” and supported reservation rates.
  • Average sales outlets (inc. JVs): 408 vs 419 (-2.6%).
  • Average sales outlets (ex JVs): 399 vs 409 (-2.4%).
  • FY26 outlet guidance reiterated: expects ~405 average sales outlets (incl. JVs) across FY26.
  • New outlet launches in quarter: 32 (including first two “synergy sales outlets” at Curborough Fields, Lichfield where Barratt Homes and David Wilson Homes outlets added to a Redrow site).
  • Synergy outlet roll-out accelerated: now “ahead of schedule”; targeting a further 6 by year-end, 22 in FY27, and remaining 15 in FY28.
  • YTD outlet launches: 97; FY27 outlook remains 425–435 average outlets (incl. JVs).
  • Planning status: detailed planning consent on 94% of plots expected to deliver FY27 completions (vs 95% for FY26 plots last year).
  • Completions (quarter): 3,274 vs 3,717 (-11.9%).
  • Completions (quarter, JV element): 57 vs 81 (-29.6%).
  • Completions (FY-to-date): 10,718 vs 10,824 (-1.0%).
  • Reason for lower quarterly completions: “particularly strong comparable quarter in FY25 ahead of the end of Stamp Duty relief.”
  • FY26 completions guidance reiterated: 17,200–17,800 (including ~600 JV completions).
  • Forward sold for FY26: 94% vs 96% last year (basis explained in note 4).
  • Total forward sales (incl. JVs) homes: 11,395 vs 10,245 (+11.2%).
  • Total forward sales (incl. JVs) value: £3,539.2m vs £3,138.6m (+12.8%, +£400.6m).
  • Private order book volume: 5,643 homes vs 5,503 (+2.5%).
  • Private order book value: £2,281.5m vs £2,243.1m (+1.7%); company notes this reflects “mix… and the impact of underlying deflation.”
  • Affordable forward sales value: £956.5m vs £745.1m (+28.4%).
  • Affordable forward sales homes: 4,889 vs 4,355 (+12.3%).
  • Wholly owned forward sales value: £3,238.0m vs £2,988.2m (+8.4%).
  • Wholly owned forward sales homes: 10,532 vs 9,858 (+6.8%).
  • JV forward sales value: £301.2m vs £150.4m (+100.3%).
  • JV forward sales homes: 863 vs 387 (+123.0%).
  • Forward sales roll (Dec to Mar, private): 4,746 → 5,643 (net +897) vs prior year 5,296 → 5,503 (net +207).
  • Forward sales roll (Dec to Mar, total): 10,440 → 11,395 (net +955) vs prior year 10,076 → 10,245 (net +169).
  • Build cost inflation guidance unchanged: ~2% for FY26 overall; ~3% anticipated in H2 FY26.
  • Cost outlook risk: “higher energy costs” likely to feed into FY27 building material costs; better visibility promised at mid-July update.
  • Cost mitigation levers cited: “enhanced purchasing scale”, long-standing supply chain relationships, disciplined cost control.
  • Construction flexibility: ability to shift from traditional to timber frame via Oregon timber frame business.
  • Quality: retained HBF five-star homebuilder status for the 17th consecutive year (described as “unparalleled industry record”).
  • Reputation: all three brands rated “Excellent” on Trustpilot.
  • Integration: operating from 32 housebuilding divisions since start of FY26 (as guided at acquisition); IT integration completes this month.
  • Synergies: £100m cost synergy target “now confirmed”.
  • Synergies delivered: £20m in FY25.
  • Synergies expected: +£50m incremental in FY26.
  • Synergies remainder: £30m to be delivered by end of December 2027.
  • Land approvals (quarter): 2,465 plots across 14 sites vs 7,574 across 37 (-67.4% plots).
  • Land approvals (FY-to-date): 4,010 plots across 29 sites vs 15,301 across 82 (-73.8% plots).
  • Land strategy: reiterated long-term goal to optimise land bank towards 3.5 years owned plots and 1.0 year controlled land.
  • Land market comment: “fewer attractive opportunities” during the period; now “even more selective” given geopolitical backdrop and mortgage/cost inflation risk.
  • Updated FY26 land approvals guidance: 7,000–9,000 plots vs prior 10,000–12,000 (midpoint down ~27%: 8,000 vs 11,000).
  • Updated FY26 land spend guidance: £700m–£800m vs prior £800m–£900m.
  • Year-end net cash guidance raised: now £550m–£650m vs prior £400m–£500m (midpoint up 33%: £600m vs £450m).
  • Reason for higher net cash guidance: timing of legacy building remediation payments + reduced land investment.
  • Share buyback: second £50m tranche started 5 January 2026.
  • Buyback deployed in period: £33.3m (through 29 March 2026).
  • Buyback shares acquired in period: 9.7m shares.
  • Cumulative FY26 buyback to date: 22.9m shares for £83.7m.
  • Buyback completion timing: remaining balance to be completed prior to end of June 2026.
  • Outlook risk framing: Middle East conflict could prolong higher-rate environment and renew cost pressures; “visibility beyond the current financial year remains more uncertain.”
  • Quote (CEO David Thomas):

"Barratt Redrow had a solid third quarter, with a resilient reservation rate underpinned by good customer demand. Despite heightened macroeconomic uncertainty, we expect the Middle East conflict to have limited impact on FY26 performance, given our strong forward sales position and advanced build programme. We are therefore on track to deliver total housing completions1 and adjusted profit before tax2 in line with consensus expectations3. "Looking ahead, we have a proven track record of navigating uncertainty and remain confident in our financial strength and ability to adapt to changing market conditions. We will continue to closely monitor developments while maintaining a disciplined approach to capital allocation, selective land investment and rigorous cost control."

summary: Q1 2026 copper production declined but net cash costs fell significantly due to higher by-product credits
body:
Time Period Q1 2026

  • Q1 net cash costs (Group): $1.08/lb.
  • Net cash costs by asset (as stated): 72c/lb at Los Pelambres and 34c/lb at Centinela.
  • Copper production (Q1 2026): 143.0kt.
  • Copper production vs Q1 2025: 154.7kt → 143.0kt (-7.6%).
  • Copper production vs Q4 2025: 177.0kt → 143.0kt (-19.2%).
  • Copper sales (Q1 2026): 137.0kt.
  • Copper sales vs Q1 2025: 170.2kt → 137.0kt (-19.5%).
  • Copper sales vs Q4 2025: 201.0kt → 137.0kt (-31.8%).
  • Gold production (Q1 2026): 46.5koz.
  • Gold production vs Q1 2025: 42.9koz → 46.5koz (+8.4%).
  • Gold production vs Q4 2025: 66.3koz → 46.5koz (-29.9%).
  • Molybdenum production (Q1 2026): 3.0kt.
  • Molybdenum production vs Q1 2025: 3.1kt → 3.0kt (-3.2%).
  • Molybdenum production vs Q4 2025: 4.4kt → 3.0kt (-31.8%).
  • Operational drivers (copper): lower processing rates + lower grades at Los Pelambres and Centinela Concentrates “in line with the mine plan”.
  • Operational drivers (gold): higher gold grades, partly offset by lower processing rates at both concentrators.
  • Cash costs before by-product credits (Q1 2026): $2.77/lb.
  • Cash costs before by-product credits vs Q1 2025: $2.37/lb → $2.77/lb (+16.9%).
  • Cash costs before by-product credits vs Q4 2025: $2.44/lb → $2.77/lb (+13.5%).
  • By-product credits (Q1 2026): $1.69/lb.
  • By-product credits YoY: +104%, driven by higher gold production and higher pricing for gold and molybdenum.
  • Net cash costs vs Q1 2025: $1.54/lb → $1.08/lb (-29.9%), driven by higher by-product credits.
  • Net cash costs vs Q4 2025: $1.05/lb → $1.08/lb (+2.9%).
  • Growth projects: “All major projects remain on track and on budget.”
  • Centinela Second Concentrator Project: 15 of 18 electrical rooms completed.
  • Centinela Second Concentrator Project: pre-commissioning commenced (recirculation tests at pumping stations).
  • Centinela Second Concentrator Project: new water infrastructure delivered its first water to Centinela as part of initial tests.
  • Los Pelambres Growth Enabling Projects – concentrate pipeline: welding, lowering-in, backfilling ongoing; tunnel-section work ongoing; 33kV electrical line installation advancing.
  • Los Pelambres Growth Enabling Projects – desalination expansion: feed/backwash pumps installed; marine works completed; structural work commenced on SWRO building.
  • Zaldívar water supply: evaluation of long-term water supply options beyond 2028 continues; decision expected during 2026.
  • Production outlook: expects copper production to increase quarter-on-quarter through 2026 via higher ore processing rates and improving grades at Los Pelambres (mine plan).
  • Strategic growth ambition stated: looking to increase copper production by 30% (via growth projects cited).
  • Guidance unchanged: full-year copper production 650–700kt.
  • Cash cost guidance unchanged: $2.30–2.50/lb (before by-product credits) and $1.15–1.35/lb (net).
  • Key assumption embedded in cost guidance: assumes fuel prices return to January 2026 levels during Q2 2026.
  • Capex guidance unchanged: $3.4bn.
  • Safety: operations remain fatality-free in 2026; LTIFR performance in line with FY2025.
  • Regulatory/water: discussions started to ensure timely annual renewal of the administrative action needed for a DGA-approved water redistribution agreement (to allow Los Pelambres to extract up to 400 l/s in drought conditions consistent with water rights).
  • Corporate update (incomplete in provided text): article states Andrónico Luksic Craig stepped down from the Board on 27 January 2026 and mentions an appointment of Andrónico Luksic Lederer as a Non-… (the remainder of this sentence was cut off in the text you provided).
  • Quote (CEO Iván Arriagada):

"We are pleased to report another strong quarter of cash cost performance. Our net cash costs during the quarter were 108c/lb at the Group level, including 72c/lb and 34c/lb at Los Pelambres and Centinela respectively, which demonstrates the quality of our portfolio, including our meaningful exposure to gold and molybdenum. "As we move through the year, we expect copper production to increase quarter-on-quarter, supported by higher ore processing rates and improving grades at Los Pelambres, in line with the mine plan. "Our key growth projects continue to advance in line with expectations. Pre-commissioning activities are now underway at the Centinela Second Concentrator Project, while progress across the Los Pelambres Growth Enabling Projects continues to strengthen the operational platform for future production growth, as we look to increase our copper production by 30%. "Against a backdrop of higher energy prices, we remain focused on safety, operational excellence, disciplined cost control and the timely execution of our growth and development projects, while maintaining resilient supply chains, and to date we have experienced no supply disruptions. The copper price remains constructive in 2026, and the medium-term fundamentals for copper are compelling, underpinned by structural demand drivers and constrained supply."

summary: Great Portland Estates achieved strong leasing growth with rents significantly ahead of valuation estimates
body:
Time Period Quarter to 31 March 2026 (with full-year leasing and disposals for the year ended 31 March 2026)

  • Quarter leasing: 28 new leases/renewals signed.
  • Quarter rent contracted: £24.4m annual rent (company share: £23.8m).
  • Quarter market lettings vs ERV: on average 15.8% ahead of March 2025 ERV.
  • Fully Managed (quarter): 20 leases signed.
  • Fully Managed (quarter) rent roll added: £14.6m.
  • Fully Managed (quarter) average rent: £233 per sq ft.
  • Fully Managed (quarter) vs ERV: 8.6% ahead of March 2025 ERV.
  • Fitted (quarter): five leases signed.
  • Fitted (quarter) rent roll added: £1.6m.
  • Fitted (quarter) vs ERV: 7.2% ahead of March 2025 ERV.
  • Significant pre-let (quarter): 52,300 sq ft to Quantexa at The Delft, SE1 (formerly Minerva House), “significantly ahead” of March 2025 ERV.
  • Quantexa lease terms: 10-year lease; floors include ground east, first east, and fifth–seventh floors.
  • The Delft delivery: 143,100 sq ft HQ workspace targeted spring 2027; includes Thames frontage and 6,000 sq ft communal roof terrace.
  • Rent reviews (quarter): seven settled (including KKR and Glencore at Hanover Square, W1).
  • Rent reviews uplift: settled 49% ahead of previous passing rent.
  • Rent reviews vs ERV: settled 11.1% ahead of valuers’ ERV at review date.
  • Full-year leasing: 88 new leases/renewals signed.
  • Full-year rent contracted: £70.9m annual rent (company share: £69.6m).
  • Full-year market lettings vs ERV: on average 10.3% ahead of March 2025 ERV.
  • Leasing pipeline: £6.4m of rent under offer.
  • Under-offer pricing vs ERV: market lettings 7.9% ahead of March 2025 ERV.
  • Fully Managed demand: reported as “strong” in prime locations.
  • SIX St Andrew Street (EC4): final remaining office space let; third and fourth floors totalling 11,680 sq ft let to an “AI-led company”.
  • SIX St Andrew Street (EC4): now fully occupied; annual rent £8.8m.
  • SIX St Andrew Street (EC4): average rent £200 per sq ft.
  • SIX St Andrew Street (EC4): 6.2% ahead of ERV; 6.2% yield on cost.
  • Largest retail letting (quarter): Kent House, W1; 14,800 sq ft regear to Reiss Limited.
  • Reiss lease terms: new 10-year lease with break at five years; described as a customer of more than 25 years.
  • Development completion: 2 Aldermanbury Square completed in March; 321,650 sq ft of premium HQ workspace.
  • 2 Aldermanbury Square: stated as GPE’s “largest office development to date”; “delivered on time and on budget”.
  • 2 Aldermanbury Square: 100% pre-let; Clifford Chance (pre-let all offices) has taken occupation to begin fit-out.
  • Evelyn Estate agreement: new headlease + development agreement to refurbish 7/15 Gresse Street and 12/13 Rathbone Place (W1).
  • Evelyn Estate headlease: term extended by 20 years, pushing expiry to 2148.
  • Evelyn Estate scope: consent to convert to Fully Managed; will provide 42,800 sq ft across five floors.
  • Flex footprint: “Flex offer” totals 654,000 sq ft.
  • Rebrand: the Gresse/Rathbone buildings to be rebranded “The Howlett”.
  • Disposal: wells&more, W1 sold to Feldberg Capital (on behalf of Fastighets AB Balder) for £172m headline price.
  • wells&more pricing: 5.0% net initial yield; £1,483 per sq ft.
  • wells&more vs book: marginally ahead of September 2025 book value; around 5% ahead of March 2025 book value.
  • Disposal: 103/113 Regent Street, W1 (53-year short leasehold interest) sold for £52m headline price.
  • 103/113 Regent Street pricing: 7.2% net initial yield; £912 per sq ft.
  • 103/113 Regent Street vs book: “marginally behind” March 2025 book value.
  • Total sales (FY): £490m, stated as 2% ahead in aggregate of respective March 2025 book values.
  • Capital recycling: proceeds being reinvested into “new West End refurbishment opportunities at a significant discount” (no %/£ given).
  • Quote (CEO Toby Courtauld):

"Despite a volatile macroeconomic backdrop, this has been an excellent finish to the year. We signed £24.4 million of leases in the quarter and delivered a record £70.9 million of deals for the year, 10.3% ahead of ERV, reflecting the strength of demand for high quality, well located space and the momentum in our Fully Managed offer. Alongside this, we completed our largest office development to date at 2 Aldermanbury Square, which is 100% pre let, and progressed our capital recycling bringing sales to some £490 million, 2% ahead of March 2025 book value, reinvesting the proceeds into new West End refurbishment opportunities at a significant discount. Looking forward, we have further leasing under offer and a strong pipeline of new space in production, and so we start the new financial year with positive momentum."

summary: Ferrexpo's 1Q 2026 production plummeted 72% due to Ukrainian infrastructure attacks and operational disruption
body:
Time Period 1Q 2026

  • Total production (1Q 2026): 593kt (592,751 tonnes in table), comprising 68kt Fe 67% premium concentrate and 525kt premium pellets.
  • Operational disruption: production activities “largely suspended” in Q1 due to nationwide attacks on Ukrainian electricity generation and transmission infrastructure.
  • Restart status: limited resumption at reduced capacity in late February 2026 after improvements in electricity availability and price.
  • Operating configuration: currently operating one of four pellet lines and selling to European customers.
  • Export logistics: continues to use its own fleet of rail wagons to export to customers in Eastern and Central Europe.
  • Safety metric: rolling 12-month LTIFR improved to 0.17 (vs historic five-year trailing average 0.44).
  • Safety record: zero workplace fatalities for more than five years.
  • Cost / liquidity actions (as per 1 April 2026 trading update, reiterated here): reduced working time, procurement cuts, continued suspension of all non-essential CapEx, overheads, and CSR spending.
  • Funding: actively exploring and progressing potential funding options, “which could include an equity capital raise.”
  • Funding risk statement: if VAT refunds withholding continues and funding issues aren’t resolved in time, “this could give rise to material negative consequences for the Group.”
  • Total commercial production vs 4Q 2025: 1,073,871 → 592,751 tonnes (-45%).
  • Total commercial production vs 1Q 2025: 2,125,467 → 592,751 tonnes (-72%).
  • Total pellet production vs 4Q 2025: 412,867 → 524,926 tonnes (+27%).
  • Total pellet production vs 1Q 2025: 1,347,749 → 524,926 tonnes (-61%).
  • DR pellets (FDP, 67% Fe): 0 (vs 81,787 tonnes in 1Q 2025; -100%).
  • Ferrexpo premium pellets (65% Fe) vs 1Q 2025: 1,105,049 → 524,926 tonnes (-52%).
  • Commercial concentrate (67% Fe) vs 4Q 2025: 661,004 → 67,825 tonnes (-90%).
  • Commercial concentrate (67% Fe) vs 1Q 2025: 777,718 → 67,825 tonnes (-91%).
  • Quote (Interim Executive Chair Lucio Genovese):

"The lower production numbers in the first quarter, at almost half of what we achieved in the final three months of 2025, reflect focused Russian attacks on Ukraine's power and energy infrastructure late last year and the resultant impact on our ability to operate sustainably. By January, given the supply of electricity could not be assured on a sustainable basis in the volumes required, we were forced to make the difficult decision to temporarily suspend operations and place a part of the workforce on furlough. Fortunately in late February we saw sufficient improvements in the availability and price of electricity to restart limited production at FPM on one pellet line. One pellet line remains in operation, and the Group continues to use its own fleet of rail wagons to export to customers in Eastern and Central Europe. We are grateful for the hard work across the country that has enabled repairs to be completed quickly. I am also grateful to colleagues who have worked tirelessly to restart the operations of one pellet line. This demonstrates, once again, our ability to be flexible and agile in a very challenging environment. Going forward, our focus remains on managing working capital and costs throughout this challenging operating environment."

summary: Hunting maintains full-year EBITDA guidance while order book surges 17% despite Q1 cash outflows
body:
Time Period Q1 2026 (quarter trading update; order book as at 14 April 2026)

  • CEO Jim Johnson said: "Hunting has delivered a solid Q1 performance, and we are maintaining our full-year EBITDA guidance of $145-$155 million given the strong outlook for our end markets.”
  • CEO Jim Johnson said: "We remain vigilant regarding Middle East volatility, with our people remaining safe and our facilities fully operational.”
  • CEO Jim Johnson said: "We are seeing excellent order book momentum across South America and the US onshore market. By restructuring our global operations and continuing our share buyback programme, we are positioning Hunting for robust, long-term growth and enhanced shareholder returns."
  • Q1 trading in line with expectations; subsea contract milestones are weighted to Q2, impacting phasing.
  • Full-year EBITDA guidance maintained at $145–$155m; earnings expected 40:60 H1:H2 (second-half weighted).
  • Q1 2026 EBITDA $23.2m; EBITDA margin 10%.
  • Perforating Systems: North America ahead of expectations, attributed to “higher quality sales and production efficiencies” improving trading.
  • Working capital build (to satisfy committed orders) drove net cash outflow; combined with buybacks, quarter-end cash/(borrowings) $8.3m vs $62.9m at 31 Dec 2025 (down $54.6m / -86.8%).
  • Sales order book (as at 14 April 2026) c.$428.8m vs $365.3m at 31 March 2026 (+17.4%) and $358.0m at 31 Dec 2025 (+19.8%).
  • OCTG tender pipeline remains c.$1.0bn; Middle East tender issuance has slipped since March 2026, but management expects publication in Q2 2026.
  • Subsea order book building post year-end; includes $63.5m titanium stress joint orders for Guyana (announced 7 April 2026).
  • Increased momentum reported in Spring, Stafford and Flexible Engineered Solutions as offshore/subsea projects execute.
  • Non-energy order book (Advanced Manufacturing) at 31 March 2026: $95.6m vs $98.6m at 31 Dec 2025 (-3.0%).
  • Non-oil & gas growth push: targeting high-spec industrial programmes with repeat demand; cited customers include Teledyne Brown Engineering, Solar Turbines, Pratt & Whitney, Sikorsky, General Electric.
  • Non-energy programmes described as multi-year production schedules, with visibility in some cases “through 2028 and beyond.”
  • Middle East: Dubai and Saudi operations/personnel safe and generally unaffected; management expects minimal impact on current-year profitability (conditional on conflict duration).
  • Despite higher commodity prices, US/international rig count not yet increasing as operators adopt “wait and see”; Hunting positioned to benefit if activity rises.
  • EMEA restructuring: Fordoun site closure on track for June 2026.
  • $15m cost reduction programme accelerating; majority of savings expected by early 2028.
  • Shared service functions (Europe & North America) expected fully operational by mid-2026, aimed at further cost/operating efficiency and lower SG&A.
  • Org change: EMEA + Asia Pacific to combine into a single “International” segment from 1 Jan 2027, to support further cost efficiencies.
  • M&A: continuing to assess bolt-on acquisitions; Subsea highlighted as a focus area.
  • Next scheduled update: H1 2026 Trading Update due Wednesday 15 July 2026.

summary: Hollywood Bowl Group reports 9.5% revenue growth with strong operational performance across markets
body:
Time Period Six months ended 31 March 2026 (H1 FY2026)

  • Group revenue £141.5m, up 9.5% vs H1 FY2025 (implies prior period c. £129.2m).
  • UK total revenue £118.4m, up 9.4% vs H1 FY2025 (implies prior period c. £108.2m).
  • Canada revenue CAD 42.9m (£23.2m), up 12.8% on a constant currency basis.
  • Group like-for-like (LFL) revenue +1.9%.
  • UK LFL +2.6%.
  • Canada LFL +0.5% (constant currency).
  • Cost/control framing: continued “disciplined cost management”; business has high gross margins and is positioned as insulated from inflation pressures.
  • Energy hedging: 76% of total electricity needs hedged until end of FY2029, including 12% from on-site solar.
  • Estate expansion: opened Edmonton (Canada) in H1; stated to be trading well.
  • Current estate: 77 UK centres and 16 Canadian centres (total 93).
  • Openings expected in H2: two new UK centres and one Canadian centre.
  • Pipeline: management highlights a strong new centre pipeline for FY2027 and beyond.
  • Capex: £8.6m invested in expansion, refurbishments and enhancements.
  • Balance sheet: net cash £26.0m at 31 March 2026; £25m revolving credit facility undrawn.
  • Outlook statement: Group remains confident for FY2026 and beyond.
  • CEO Stephen Burns said: "The benefits of the investments made throughout the UK and Canadian estate, combined with proactive demand generation initiatives and disciplined cost management, are reflected in our strong H1 performance. Demand for high-quality, family leisure activities that offer great value for money also remains resilient in both territories, and our cash generative business model allows us to invest where we see opportunities and deliver profitable growth."

Hunting PLC (duplicate / repeat of the earlier Hunting Q1 2026 trading update text) Time Period Q1 2026 (quarter trading update; order book as at 14 April 2026)

  • Repeats the same core disclosures as the earlier Hunting update: EBITDA guidance $145–$155m maintained; Q1 EBITDA $23.2m (10% margin); cash/(borrowings) $8.3m vs $62.9m at 31 Dec 2025; order book $428.8m as at 14 April 2026; OCTG pipeline c.$1.0bn; $63.5m Guyana titanium stress joint order; Fordoun closure June 2026; $15m cost reduction programme; EMEA + Asia Pacific combining into “International” from 1 Jan 2027; bolt-on M&A focus on Subsea.
  • Repeats the CEO quote in full: "Hunting has delivered a solid Q1 performance, and we are maintaining our full-year EBITDA guidance of $145-$155 million given the strong outlook for our end markets… We remain vigilant regarding Middle East volatility… We are seeing excellent order book momentum across South America and the US onshore market…"

summary: Standard Life to acquire Aegon UK for £2.0bn, creating largest UK retirement business
body:
Time Period Transaction announcement (metrics referenced mainly FY2025; completion expected around end-2026)

  • Announced agreement to acquire Aegon UK plc (UK insurance & pensions ops of Aegon Europe Holding B.V.) for total consideration of £2.0bn.
  • Funding mix: debt + cash + new shares; Standard Life will issue new ordinary shares to Aegon representing ~15.3% of enlarged share capital on completion.
  • Cash component: £750m cash consideration, financed via £650m debt issuance prior to completion plus cash resources.
  • Equity component: 181.1m newly issued Standard Life shares to Aegon at completion; priced using 30-day VWAP of 690p (as at time of binding offer).
  • Aegon becomes a 15.3% shareholder; lock-up ends at earlier of 18 months post-completion or Aegon ultimate holding company re-domiciliation to the US (expected 1 January 2028).
  • Relationship agreement: Aegon entitled to appoint a non-executive director; also underpins a strategic asset management partnership.
  • Transaction expected to complete around end 2026, subject to regulatory conditions.
  • Positioning claim: combined business described as the UK’s largest retirement savings and income business, with ~16m customers and c.£480bn AUA.
  • Market ranking claims: combined group to become #2 in UK Workplace and #2 in UK Retail (by the measures referenced in the release).
  • Valuation: 0.83x Price/UT1, based on Aegon UK UT1 £2.4bn (FY2025).
  • Synergies / value: total net synergy value £0.8bn, comprising:

£110m run-rate pre-tax cost synergies (recurring). c.£340m one-off capital synergies.

  • Delivery timing on cost synergies: over half by end-2029, remainder by end-2031.
  • One-offs: ~£0.3bn post-tax integration costs plus £0.1bn additional post-tax separation costs.
  • Cash impact: expected to increase Operating Cash Generation by £160m per annum; also cites £0.4bn incremental excess cash over 5 years after financing and one-off costs.
  • Earnings impact: expected to add ~£190m IFRS adjusted operating profit per annum and be mid-single digit accretive to adjusted operating EPS by 2029.
  • Capital/leverage: funding consistent with existing Solvency II leverage target ~30%; states intention to maintain solvency coverage at the upper end of range.
  • On completion: anticipated single-digit uplift to Shareholder Capital Coverage Ratio (SCCR) and £1.25bn increase in IFRS shareholders’ equity.
  • Dividend policy: said to further underpin progressive and sustainable dividend on a per-share basis.
  • Strategic mix shift: operating profit contribution from capital-light fee-based growth earnings increases from 47% (FY25 standalone) to 57% for enlarged group (pro-forma post synergy realisation) (+10pp).
  • Workplace detail: adds £74bn AUA and 2.1m customers to Standard Life’s existing £71bn workplace offering; cites £18bn pro-forma gross annual flows (year ended 31 Dec 2025).
  • Retail detail: adds £86bn AUA and 1.8m customers; cites £12bn pro-forma gross annual flows (year ended 31 Dec 2025).
  • Group-wide uplift: adds ~£160bn AUA and 3.8m customers to create ~£480bn AUA / 16m customers.
  • Annuities capital allocation unchanged: commitment to allocate £200m capital per annum to annuities remains; continues exploring capital-light participation in pension risk transfer.
  • Support noted from existing strategic shareholders MS&AD Insurance Group Holdings, Inc and Aberdeen Group Plc (stated supportive and committed to shareholdings).
  • Standard Life CEO Andy Briggs said: "Our agreement to acquire Aegon UK significantly accelerates our vision to be the UK's leading retirement savings and income business. We will be in an even stronger position to meet the evolving needs of our 16 million customers with enhanced digital, advice and distribution capabilities across Workplace and Retail, strengthening our standing in one of the world's most attractive markets. Furthermore, the transaction accelerates our shift to capital-light whilst strengthening our cash, capital and earnings position to create increased value for shareholders.

summary: Aegon CEO announces Standard Life as the right owner for Aegon UK operations
body: • Aegon CEO Lard Friese said: "Standard Life is the right owner for Aegon UK: we share the same values and a strong commitment to customers, and together the businesses will create the UK's largest retirement savings and income provider. The businesses are complementary and the combination offers an excellent outcome for Aegon UK's customers and colleagues. Aegon's shareholding will provide an opportunity to participate in the future success of the enlarged group."

summary: PPHE Hotel Group will release Q1 2026 trading update on April 29
body:
Time Period Three months ended 31 March 2026 (update scheduled for release)

  • Issued a notice that it will announce a trading update for the three months ended 31 March 2026 on Wednesday 29 April 2026.
  • No financial or trading metrics disclosed in this notice.

summary: SELP joint venture priced €500m five-year bond at 3.875% with strong demand
body:
Time Period Bond pricing announcement (dated 15 April 2026)

  • SELP joint venture (SEGRO acting as venture adviser) priced a €500m senior unsecured bond issue.
  • Bond tenor: 5 years.
  • Pricing: 105 bps above euro mid-swaps, equating to an annual coupon of 3.875%.
  • Demand: order book described as more than 10x covered at peak.

summary: Victorian Plumbing launches four-year value creation plan linking executive pay to share price milestones
body:
Time Period Value creation plan with a four-year time horizon

  • Announces a value creation plan to “align executive leadership rewards directly with the delivery of sustained shareholder value”.
  • Updates the executive board’s long-term incentive arrangement via a one-off award “designed to incentivise the pursuit of strategic growth opportunities”.
  • Plan design links (and increases) executive compensation to share price milestone achievement.
  • Executive board “value creation pool” rises to GBP12.3 million if the share price reaches GBP2.75.
  • Executive board “value creation pool” rises to GBP20.5 million if the share price reaches GBP3.0.
  • The GBP20.5 million pool is GBP8.2 million higher than the GBP12.3 million pool (+66.7%).
  • CEO Stephnie Judge receives 25% of the overall incentive pool regardless of share price (implies GBP3.075 million at the GBP12.3m pool; GBP5.125 million at the GBP20.5m pool).
  • CFO Daniel Barton receives 20% of the overall incentive pool regardless of share price (implies GBP2.46 million at the GBP12.3m pool; GBP4.10 million at the GBP20.5m pool).
  • Implied remaining allocation to other executive board members is 55% of the overall pool (based on the stated CEO/CFO fixed percentages).

summary: Hunting PLC wins $63.5 million titanium stress joint order for Guyana offshore development
body:
Time Period Order delivery through May 2028; revenue recognition from H2 2026

  • Awarded $63.5 million of orders for its titanium stress joint (TSJ) product line for a new offshore development in Guyana.
  • Award to be completed by the Group’s Subsea Spring business unit.
  • Deliveries scheduled through to May 2028.
  • Revenue expected to be recognised from H2 2026.
  • New TSJ order is in addition to $4.4 million of orders already secured for the same development since December 2025 (via Stafford and Flexible Engineered Solutions).
  • Total orders disclosed for this development since December 2025 are $67.9 million ($63.5m + $4.4m).
  • The $63.5m TSJ order is $59.1m higher than the $4.4m previously disclosed (+1,343%, or ~14.4x).
  • Company signals “further incremental orders likely to be received throughout the rest of 2026.”
  • Positions TSJ adoption on FPSO projects as supporting a strategy to capture more subsea spend by leveraging an enlarged product offering across the well life cycle.
  • CEO Jim Johnson said:“Our TSJ product line, which is a critical component for offshore developments, continues to be adopted on key offshore projects utilising FPSOs, given the maintenance benefits and reliability offered.

“We would like to thank our partner in Guyana for its continued confidence in our product offering and look forward to working together in the future with our range of products and solutions. “This order also contributes to our guided subsea product group revenue and EBITDA through to 2028, as published at our subsea investor event held in January 2026.”

summary: Wix repurchased 17.6 million shares at $92 per share for $1.617 billion
body:
Time Period Modified Dutch Auction tender offer expired April 1, 2026

  • Announced final results of its “modified Dutch Auction” tender offer, which expired “at one (1) minute after 11:59 P.M., New York City time, on April 1, 2026.”
  • Deposit count (Equiniti Trust Company, LLC): 17,577,250 ordinary shares were properly tendered and not properly withdrawn at or below $92.00 per share.
  • Notes that not all shares tendered via notice of guaranteed delivery were delivered within the required settlement period.
  • Accepted for payment 17,577,250 shares (all properly tendered and not properly withdrawn) at $92.00 per share.
  • Aggregate repurchase cost stated as ~$1.617 billion, excluding fees and expenses.
  • Repurchased shares represent ~29.7% of shares outstanding as of April 1, 2026.
  • Implied total shares outstanding as of April 1, 2026 are ~59.2 million (based on 17.577m being 29.7%).
  • Nir Zohar, President and Co-founder, stated: “I am very pleased with the outcome of our Dutch auction tender offer, which is enabling us to repurchase nearly 30% of our market capitalization and return meaningful value to investors. We believe the results of this transaction reflect shared conviction among our broader shareholder base in our long-term strateg”

summary: Lloyds maintains £1.95bn motor finance provision after FCA finalises reduced redress scheme
body:
Time Period Motor finance redress scheme update (as at 2 April 2026)

  • Provision for the motor finance issue remains GBP1.95bn; Lloyds said it has “undertaken an assessment of the implications and impact of the final rules” and that “the group does not currently believe any change to the provision for this issue is required,” ()
  • FCA confirmed the industry-wide motor finance redress scheme on 30 March 2026. ()
  • FCA eligibility estimate tightened to 12.1m agreements (from 14.2m at consultation; -14.8%). ()
  • FCA expected total compensation (“redress”) GBP7.5bn (from GBP8.2bn at consultation; -8.5%) and total industry cost (incl. admin) GBP9.1bn (from GBP11.0bn; -17.3%). ()
  • FCA says the scheme is being streamlined (incl. not requiring recorded delivery), claiming it cuts delivery cost to firms by >40%; also notes “in around 1 in 3 cases compensation will be capped.” ()
  • Lloyds flagged residual uncertainty (response rates, litigation, operational costs): “The ultimate outcome may also differ dependent upon potential actions by various parties, including legal proceedings and complaints. An update will be provided as appropriate with the first quarter results at the end of April,” ()
  • Lloyds Q1 results date: 29 April 2026. ()
  • Share move on the day: -1.6% to 96.18p, but up >5% over the week (per the report). ()
  • Shore Capital view (via Proactive): stance “appears reasonable” given FCA tweaks, but the key risks remain litigation and take-up; Shore also highlights the FCA’s assumed participation rate moving to 75% from 85% (-11.8%) and estimates expected remediation costs for banks down to ~GBP5.2bn from ~GBP5.6bn (non-banks benefiting more). ()
  • Earlier Lloyds positioning after FCA decision: “The details of the final scheme differ from the scheme as laid out in October 2025 and require careful analysis. Accordingly, the group is assessing the implications and impact of the final rules. The group will update the market as and when appropriate,” ()
  • External/legal overhang cited in press: Black Horse exposure includes a reported lawsuit involving ~30,000 customers seeking GBP66m (per reporting referenced alongside this update). ()

summary: Ryanair reports strong March traffic growth but warns of potential supply disruption risks from ongoing conflict
body:
Time Period March 2026 traffic statistics (and rolling 12 months to end-March 2026)

  • March passengers 15.8m (+5.3% YoY); load factor 93% (flat YoY). ()
  • March flights operated: 88,000+. ()
  • Rolling 12-month passengers 208.4m (+4.1% YoY); load factor 94% (flat YoY). ()
  • O’Leary (Sky News) on timing/risk of supply disruption: “But if the war continues, we do run the risk of supply disruptions in Europe in May and June and obviously we hope the war will finish sooner than that and that the risk to supply will be eliminated,” ()
  • O’Leary on scenario outcomes: “It’s almost impossible to know. If the war finishes and the Strait of Hormuz is opened by the middle or end of April, then there’s no risk to supply.” ()
  • O’Leary on potential scale of impact: “If the war continues, and the disruption to supply continues, we think there is a reasonable risk that maybe 10%, 20%, 25% of our supplies might be at risk through May and June.” ()
  • O’Leary concluding: “So, like everybody else in the industry, we hope this war ends sooner rather than later.” ()
  • Shares (early Thursday): -1.6% to EUR24.56. ()
  • Additional context from Reuters: Ryanair has warned prolonged conflict could force summer capacity reductions/cancellations if fuel availability tightens; it has also pointed to hedging and monitoring suppliers closely. ()

summary: Wizz Air March traffic grew 8.4% YoY despite slight load factor decline and capacity expansion
body:
Time Period March 2026 traffic and CO₂ emission statistics (and rolling 12 months to end-March 2026)

  • March passengers 5.5m (+8.4% YoY). ()
  • March capacity +9.1% YoY to 6.12m seats. ()
  • March load factor 90.0% (from 90.5%; -0.5ppts YoY). ()
  • Rolling 12-month passengers 69.7m (+10% YoY). ()
  • Rolling 12-month load factor 90.7% (from 91.2%; -0.5ppts YoY). ()
  • CO₂ emissions per passenger-km: -2.7% YoY in March; -3.1% on a rolling 12-month basis. ()
  • Network/ops: continued expansion in Italy (adding aircraft for Milan/Rome/Venice) and opened a base at Podgorica (Montenegro). ()
  • Wizz made no comment on fuel supply risk in this update. ()
  • Shares (early Thursday): -4.0% to 866.50p. ()

summary: Berkeley Group executives buy shares despite halting land purchases due to housing market downturn
body:
Time Period Director dealings (shares bought on 1 April 2026; reported 2 April 2026)

  • Jane Eady (wife of CFO Neil Eady) bought 1,750 shares at GBP28.86 (value GBP50,497). ()
  • Executive Chair Robert Perrins bought 7,000 shares at GBP28.78 (value GBP201,482). ()
  • Share weakness catalyst cited: Berkeley said it has decided to halt land buying amid a UK housing market downturn it linked to the Middle East conflict; it also cited an “unprecedented” increase in UK cost/regulation. ()
  • Reported reference prices: 3,134.00p (+1.0% on the Thursday); 12-month change -12%. ()

summary: Peel Hunt downgrades Harbour Energy to hold despite stable production outlook through 2027
body:
Time Period Broker note / forward outlook (2026–2027)

  • Peel Hunt downgraded to ‘hold’ from ‘buy’, with price target unchanged at 270p. ()
  • Broker rationale in the note you provided: upgrade to medium-term production outlook (stable 2026; +3% growth in 2027 vs prior), but rating cut after a sharp share-price move.
  • Related company context (for anchoring): Harbour reported record 2025 production of 474 kboepd (+84% YoY) and refreshed its shareholder returns framework (linking returns to free cash flow/leverage). ()

summary: Barbara Moorhouse steps down as Non-executive Director at Balfour Beatty on 31 May 2026
body:
Time Period Board change effective 31 May 2026

  • Barbara Moorhouse will step down as a Non-executive Director on 31 May 2026 after nine years on the board. ()
  • Chair quote (Charles Allen, Lord Allen of Kensington, CBE): “On behalf of the Board, I would like to thank Barbara for her outstanding contribution to Balfour Beatty over the past nine years. Barbara has brought great insight, rigour and sound judgement to her role, and her service on the Audit and Risk, Remuneration and Nomination Committees has been highly valued. We are grateful for her significant commitment and dedication, and we wish her every success in the future.” ()

summary: Marie Wall departed as Interim CFO on 2 April 2026 with standard leaver benefits
body:
Time Period Interim CFO departure (effective 2 April 2026) and leaver terms (FY ending June 2026)

  • Marie Wall stepped down from the Board and as Interim CFO on 2 April 2026 (following the 20 Jan 2026 announcement). ()
  • Marie received salary, pension and benefits up to 2 April 2026, plus a payment in lieu of accrued but untaken holiday. ()
  • Annual bonus: treated as a good leaver; eligible for an annual pro-rata bonus for the financial year ending June 2026, subject to Remuneration Committee approval, paid at the normal payment date. ()
  • Share plan: awards under the DFS Group Share Plan lapse on leaving; includes a grant of 182,724 shares. ()
  • Disclosure: remuneration details to be included in the Directors’ Remuneration Report in the Annual Report for the year ending 28 June 2026; the Section 430(2B) info remains on the website until the next Remuneration Report is published. ()

summary: LGI Homes closed 916 homes in Q1 2026, including 35 rental properties
body:
Time Period March 2026 and Q1 2026 home closings (as at 31 March 2026)

  • March 2026 closings: 451 homes, including 9 currently/previously leased single-family rental homes (~2.0% of March closings). ()
  • Q1 2026 closings: 916 homes, including 35 currently/previously leased single-family rental homes (~3.8% of Q1 closings). ()

summary: Saltchuk acquires Great Lakes Dredge & Dock for $1.5 billion in all-cash deal
body:
Time Period Transaction closed 01 April 2026; tender offer expired late 31 March 2026 ()

  • Acquisition closed: Great Lakes became Saltchuk’s newest wholly owned subsidiary; $17.00/share cash, enterprise value ~$1.5bn. ()
  • Quote (Mark Tabbutt, Saltchuk Chairman): “We're proud to welcome Great Lakes to the Saltchuk family of companies… With Great Lakes, we have grown to nearly 10,000 team members… We look forward to supporting Great Lakes’ reinvestment and growth ambitions for generations to come.” ()
  • Operating model: Great Lakes to join as a stand-alone business unit, continuing to operate independently under current leadership. ()
  • Quote (Lasse Petterson, GLDD CEO): “Joining Saltchuk’s family of companies is a proud moment for us… This partnership represents a natural alignment of values and vision…”
  • Quote (Petterson – strategy): “As we look ahead, we remain focused on executing our long-term growth strategy with discipline and purpose… committed to maintaining and enhancing our leadership position in U.S. dredging, the global offshore energy sector…”
  • Tender offer expiry: one minute after 11:59pm New York time on 31 March 2026. ()
  • Shares tendered: 53,738,558 shares tendered and not withdrawn = 79.88% of issued/outstanding GLDD shares. ()
  • Listing impact: before NASDAQ open on 01 April 2026, GLDD shares cease trading and then are delisted and deregistered. ()

summary: Intel repurchases Apollo's 49% Fab 34 stake for $14.2bn at 26.8% premium
body:
Time Period Agreement announced 01 April 2026 (repurchase financing/benefits discussed through 2027+) ()

  • Intel to repurchase the 49% JV equity interest in Fab 34 (Ireland) not held by Intel for $14.2bn. ()
  • Implied uplift vs 2024 structure: Apollo invested $11.2bn in 2024 for the 49% stake; Intel repurchase price implies a ~26.8% premium vs that headline amount (14.2 vs 11.2). ()
  • Funding: cash on hand + ~$6.5bn of new debt issuance. ()
  • Intel impact: expected accretive to ongoing EPS; expected to strengthen Intel’s credit profile “in 2027 and beyond”; Intel still expects to retire maturities as due in 2026–2027. ()
  • Quote (David Zinsner, Intel CFO): “We thank Apollo for their ongoing partnership… Our 2024 agreement was the right structure at the right time… Today, we have a stronger balance sheet, improved financial discipline and an evolved business strategy…”
  • Quote (Jamshid Ehsani, Apollo Partner): “Flexibility and alignment are core to how we approach relationships… we are pleased to facilitate this transaction in support of Intel’s evolving strategic and operational priorities…”
  • Operational footprint note: Fab 34 described as a high-volume facility for Intel 4 / Intel 3 products (including Intel Core Ultra and Intel Xeon 6). ()

summary: Concrete Pumping acquires UK temporary power provider Templant to diversify service offerings
body:
Time Period Acquisition announcement (strategic entry into UK temporary power) ()

  • Concrete Pumping (via Camfaud) acquired Templant Hire Limited, entering the UK temporary power market and positioning it as an adjacent-sector platform. ()
  • Templant asset base: 250+ generators spanning 20 kVA to 1250 kVA, plus distribution equipment, cabling, fuel management, and on-site support. ()
  • Strategic fit: intended to complement Camfaud’s UK concrete pumping footprint and broaden offering across construction/infrastructure customers. ()
  • Synergy plan explicitly flagged: cross-selling, shared customer relationships, and using Camfaud’s national footprint to support Templant expansion. ()
  • Financial impact framing: transaction expected to be net debt neutral. ()
  • Quote (Bruce Young, President & CEO): “This acquisition represents an important step in executing our strategy to build a diversified, multi-service platform… we see clear opportunities to accelerate growth… while also delivering meaningful synergies and long-term sustainable value…”
  • Quote (Tony Faud, MD, U.K. Camfaud Group): “We are delighted to be partnering with Templant… This acquisition enhances our ability to support customers with a broader range of solutions…”
  • Quote (Chris Allen, MD, Templant): “Joining forces with Camfaud provides a platform to accelerate our growth, expand our geographic reach, and continue delivering high-quality solutions to our

summary: Renew Holdings reports strong H1 growth with record order book despite some operational challenges
body:
Time Period Six months ended 31 March 2026 (H1). ()

  • Board expects trading and net cash in line with expectations for H1. ()
  • Company-compiled analyst consensus (H1): adjusted revenue £1,185.8m (~£1.19bn). ()
  • Company-compiled analyst consensus (H1): adjusted operating profit £77.6m. ()
  • Company-compiled analyst consensus (H1): pre-IFRS 16 net cash £20.0m. ()
