summary: Thérèse Esperdy appointed Chair of Rentokil Initial, succeeding Richard Solomons from September 2026
body:
Time Period N/A (Board succession / appointments effective July–September 2026)

  • Appointment: Thérèse Esperdy appointed Chair of the Board, commencing 1 September 2026.
  • Board entry: Esperdy will join as Non-Executive Director and Chair-designate on 1 July 2026.
  • Succession context: Follows 5 March 2026 announcement that current Chair Richard Solomons (Chair since 2019) intended to retire.
  • Current external roles (at announcement): Non-Executive Chair of Imperial Brands plc (to retire following its November 2026 Board meeting and FY2026 results announcement), Senior Independent Director designate at Smith & Nephew plc, and Non-Executive Director at Moody’s Corporation.
  • Prior non-exec role: Previously Senior Independent Director of National Grid plc.
  • Executive background: Senior leadership roles at JP Morgan including Global Chair of Financial Institutions Group, Co-head of Asia-Pacific Corporate & Investment Banking, and Global Head of Debt Capital Markets.
  • Governance: Board determined Esperdy will be independent on appointment under the UK Corporate Governance Code.
  • Committee role: Esperdy to succeed Solomons as Chair of the Nomination Committee on 1 September 2026.
  • Remuneration: Esperdy will receive total fees of £725,000 per annum.
  • Outgoing Chair departure date: Richard Solomons will retire from the Board effective midnight on 31 August 2026.
  • Listing disclosures: Company states no additional details to disclose under Listing Rule 6.4.8 in respect of the appointment.
  • Quote (Senior Independent Director, John Pettigrew):

"We are delighted to welcome Thérèse to Rentokil Initial. She brings considerable experience to the Board from her wide-ranging non-executive roles in international businesses based in both the UK and the US. The Board is confident that she is the best possible candidate to lead the Board through the exciting next phase of Rentokil Initial's growth, working with Mike and the executive team. "On behalf of the Board, I would like to thank Richard for his strong, committed leadership and his significant contributions to the Company over many years."

  • Quote (Thérèse Esperdy, incoming Chair):

"It's a privilege to be appointed as Chair of the Board, and I am looking forward to working with my new colleagues to drive the Company forward and realise the great potential it has as a leading global provider of pest control and hygiene services."

  • Quote (Richard Solomons, outgoing Chair):

"It has been an immense privilege to serve as Chair of Rentokil Initial over what has been a period of growth and transformation for the business. With the successful appointment of Mike Duffy as CEO, and a clear strategy developed for North American growth, 2026 is the right time for me to step down and ensure a smooth handover to Thérèse. I have great confidence in the future success of Rentokil Initial - it's a high-quality business, full of exceptional people, operating in highly resilient growth markets."

summary: Barratt Redrow posts solid Q3 with resilient reservation rates and on-track guidance
body:
Time Period 13 weeks from 29 December 2025 to 29 March 2026 (Q3 FY26 trading update)

  • CEO framing: “solid third quarter” with “resilient reservation rate” and “good customer demand”.
  • Macro view: expects Middle East conflict to have limited impact on FY26 performance due to “strong forward sales position and advanced build programme”.
  • Guidance: still on track to deliver total housing completions and adjusted PBT in line with consensus.
  • Consensus (compiled 14 April 2026): FY26 adjusted PBT £568m (range £534m–£586m).
  • Consensus (compiled 14 April 2026): FY26 completions (incl. JVs) 17,416 (range 16,998–17,589).
  • Net private reservation rate (ex PRS/MUS): 0.64 vs 0.62 (+3.2%).
  • Net private reservation rate (incl. PRS/MUS): 0.67 vs 0.63 (+6.3%).
  • PRS/MUS contribution to reservation rate: 0.03 vs 0.01 (+200%, i.e., +0.02 absolute).
  • Sales incentives: “in line with HY26” and supported reservation rates.
  • Average sales outlets (inc. JVs): 408 vs 419 (-2.6%).
  • Average sales outlets (ex JVs): 399 vs 409 (-2.4%).
  • FY26 outlet guidance reiterated: expects ~405 average sales outlets (incl. JVs) across FY26.
  • New outlet launches in quarter: 32 (including first two “synergy sales outlets” at Curborough Fields, Lichfield where Barratt Homes and David Wilson Homes outlets added to a Redrow site).
  • Synergy outlet roll-out accelerated: now “ahead of schedule”; targeting a further 6 by year-end, 22 in FY27, and remaining 15 in FY28.
  • YTD outlet launches: 97; FY27 outlook remains 425–435 average outlets (incl. JVs).
  • Planning status: detailed planning consent on 94% of plots expected to deliver FY27 completions (vs 95% for FY26 plots last year).
  • Completions (quarter): 3,274 vs 3,717 (-11.9%).
  • Completions (quarter, JV element): 57 vs 81 (-29.6%).
  • Completions (FY-to-date): 10,718 vs 10,824 (-1.0%).
  • Reason for lower quarterly completions: “particularly strong comparable quarter in FY25 ahead of the end of Stamp Duty relief.”
  • FY26 completions guidance reiterated: 17,200–17,800 (including ~600 JV completions).
  • Forward sold for FY26: 94% vs 96% last year (basis explained in note 4).
  • Total forward sales (incl. JVs) homes: 11,395 vs 10,245 (+11.2%).
  • Total forward sales (incl. JVs) value: £3,539.2m vs £3,138.6m (+12.8%, +£400.6m).
  • Private order book volume: 5,643 homes vs 5,503 (+2.5%).
  • Private order book value: £2,281.5m vs £2,243.1m (+1.7%); company notes this reflects “mix… and the impact of underlying deflation.”
  • Affordable forward sales value: £956.5m vs £745.1m (+28.4%).
  • Affordable forward sales homes: 4,889 vs 4,355 (+12.3%).
  • Wholly owned forward sales value: £3,238.0m vs £2,988.2m (+8.4%).
  • Wholly owned forward sales homes: 10,532 vs 9,858 (+6.8%).
  • JV forward sales value: £301.2m vs £150.4m (+100.3%).
  • JV forward sales homes: 863 vs 387 (+123.0%).
  • Forward sales roll (Dec to Mar, private): 4,746 → 5,643 (net +897) vs prior year 5,296 → 5,503 (net +207).
  • Forward sales roll (Dec to Mar, total): 10,440 → 11,395 (net +955) vs prior year 10,076 → 10,245 (net +169).
  • Build cost inflation guidance unchanged: ~2% for FY26 overall; ~3% anticipated in H2 FY26.
  • Cost outlook risk: “higher energy costs” likely to feed into FY27 building material costs; better visibility promised at mid-July update.
  • Cost mitigation levers cited: “enhanced purchasing scale”, long-standing supply chain relationships, disciplined cost control.
  • Construction flexibility: ability to shift from traditional to timber frame via Oregon timber frame business.
  • Quality: retained HBF five-star homebuilder status for the 17th consecutive year (described as “unparalleled industry record”).
  • Reputation: all three brands rated “Excellent” on Trustpilot.
  • Integration: operating from 32 housebuilding divisions since start of FY26 (as guided at acquisition); IT integration completes this month.
  • Synergies: £100m cost synergy target “now confirmed”.
  • Synergies delivered: £20m in FY25.
  • Synergies expected: +£50m incremental in FY26.
  • Synergies remainder: £30m to be delivered by end of December 2027.
  • Land approvals (quarter): 2,465 plots across 14 sites vs 7,574 across 37 (-67.4% plots).
  • Land approvals (FY-to-date): 4,010 plots across 29 sites vs 15,301 across 82 (-73.8% plots).
  • Land strategy: reiterated long-term goal to optimise land bank towards 3.5 years owned plots and 1.0 year controlled land.
  • Land market comment: “fewer attractive opportunities” during the period; now “even more selective” given geopolitical backdrop and mortgage/cost inflation risk.
  • Updated FY26 land approvals guidance: 7,000–9,000 plots vs prior 10,000–12,000 (midpoint down ~27%: 8,000 vs 11,000).
  • Updated FY26 land spend guidance: £700m–£800m vs prior £800m–£900m.
  • Year-end net cash guidance raised: now £550m–£650m vs prior £400m–£500m (midpoint up 33%: £600m vs £450m).
  • Reason for higher net cash guidance: timing of legacy building remediation payments + reduced land investment.
  • Share buyback: second £50m tranche started 5 January 2026.
  • Buyback deployed in period: £33.3m (through 29 March 2026).
  • Buyback shares acquired in period: 9.7m shares.
  • Cumulative FY26 buyback to date: 22.9m shares for £83.7m.
  • Buyback completion timing: remaining balance to be completed prior to end of June 2026.
  • Outlook risk framing: Middle East conflict could prolong higher-rate environment and renew cost pressures; “visibility beyond the current financial year remains more uncertain.”
  • Quote (CEO David Thomas):

"Barratt Redrow had a solid third quarter, with a resilient reservation rate underpinned by good customer demand. Despite heightened macroeconomic uncertainty, we expect the Middle East conflict to have limited impact on FY26 performance, given our strong forward sales position and advanced build programme. We are therefore on track to deliver total housing completions1 and adjusted profit before tax2 in line with consensus expectations3. "Looking ahead, we have a proven track record of navigating uncertainty and remain confident in our financial strength and ability to adapt to changing market conditions. We will continue to closely monitor developments while maintaining a disciplined approach to capital allocation, selective land investment and rigorous cost control."

summary: Antofagasta Q1 2026 copper production fell but net cash costs declined to $1.08/lb
body:
Time Period Q1 2026

  • Q1 net cash costs (Group): $1.08/lb.
  • Net cash costs by asset (as stated): 72c/lb at Los Pelambres and 34c/lb at Centinela.
  • Copper production (Q1 2026): 143.0kt.
  • Copper production vs Q1 2025: 154.7kt → 143.0kt (-7.6%).
  • Copper production vs Q4 2025: 177.0kt → 143.0kt (-19.2%).
  • Copper sales (Q1 2026): 137.0kt.
  • Copper sales vs Q1 2025: 170.2kt → 137.0kt (-19.5%).
  • Copper sales vs Q4 2025: 201.0kt → 137.0kt (-31.8%).
  • Gold production (Q1 2026): 46.5koz.
  • Gold production vs Q1 2025: 42.9koz → 46.5koz (+8.4%).
  • Gold production vs Q4 2025: 66.3koz → 46.5koz (-29.9%).
  • Molybdenum production (Q1 2026): 3.0kt.
  • Molybdenum production vs Q1 2025: 3.1kt → 3.0kt (-3.2%).
  • Molybdenum production vs Q4 2025: 4.4kt → 3.0kt (-31.8%).
  • Operational drivers (copper): lower processing rates + lower grades at Los Pelambres and Centinela Concentrates “in line with the mine plan”.
  • Operational drivers (gold): higher gold grades, partly offset by lower processing rates at both concentrators.
  • Cash costs before by-product credits (Q1 2026): $2.77/lb.
  • Cash costs before by-product credits vs Q1 2025: $2.37/lb → $2.77/lb (+16.9%).
  • Cash costs before by-product credits vs Q4 2025: $2.44/lb → $2.77/lb (+13.5%).
  • By-product credits (Q1 2026): $1.69/lb.
  • By-product credits YoY: +104%, driven by higher gold production and higher pricing for gold and molybdenum.
  • Net cash costs vs Q1 2025: $1.54/lb → $1.08/lb (-29.9%), driven by higher by-product credits.
  • Net cash costs vs Q4 2025: $1.05/lb → $1.08/lb (+2.9%).
  • Growth projects: “All major projects remain on track and on budget.”
  • Centinela Second Concentrator Project: 15 of 18 electrical rooms completed.
  • Centinela Second Concentrator Project: pre-commissioning commenced (recirculation tests at pumping stations).
  • Centinela Second Concentrator Project: new water infrastructure delivered its first water to Centinela as part of initial tests.
  • Los Pelambres Growth Enabling Projects – concentrate pipeline: welding, lowering-in, backfilling ongoing; tunnel-section work ongoing; 33kV electrical line installation advancing.
  • Los Pelambres Growth Enabling Projects – desalination expansion: feed/backwash pumps installed; marine works completed; structural work commenced on SWRO building.
  • Zaldívar water supply: evaluation of long-term water supply options beyond 2028 continues; decision expected during 2026.
  • Production outlook: expects copper production to increase quarter-on-quarter through 2026 via higher ore processing rates and improving grades at Los Pelambres (mine plan).
  • Strategic growth ambition stated: looking to increase copper production by 30% (via growth projects cited).
  • Guidance unchanged: full-year copper production 650–700kt.
  • Cash cost guidance unchanged: $2.30–2.50/lb (before by-product credits) and $1.15–1.35/lb (net).
  • Key assumption embedded in cost guidance: assumes fuel prices return to January 2026 levels during Q2 2026.
  • Capex guidance unchanged: $3.4bn.
  • Safety: operations remain fatality-free in 2026; LTIFR performance in line with FY2025.
  • Regulatory/water: discussions started to ensure timely annual renewal of the administrative action needed for a DGA-approved water redistribution agreement (to allow Los Pelambres to extract up to 400 l/s in drought conditions consistent with water rights).
  • Corporate update (incomplete in provided text): article states Andrónico Luksic Craig stepped down from the Board on 27 January 2026 and mentions an appointment of Andrónico Luksic Lederer as a Non-… (the remainder of this sentence was cut off in the text you provided).
  • Quote (CEO Iván Arriagada):

"We are pleased to report another strong quarter of cash cost performance. Our net cash costs during the quarter were 108c/lb at the Group level, including 72c/lb and 34c/lb at Los Pelambres and Centinela respectively, which demonstrates the quality of our portfolio, including our meaningful exposure to gold and molybdenum. "As we move through the year, we expect copper production to increase quarter-on-quarter, supported by higher ore processing rates and improving grades at Los Pelambres, in line with the mine plan. "Our key growth projects continue to advance in line with expectations. Pre-commissioning activities are now underway at the Centinela Second Concentrator Project, while progress across the Los Pelambres Growth Enabling Projects continues to strengthen the operational platform for future production growth, as we look to increase our copper production by 30%. "Against a backdrop of higher energy prices, we remain focused on safety, operational excellence, disciplined cost control and the timely execution of our growth and development projects, while maintaining resilient supply chains, and to date we have experienced no supply disruptions. The copper price remains constructive in 2026, and the medium-term fundamentals for copper are compelling, underpinned by structural demand drivers and constrained supply."

summary: Great Portland Estates reports strong Q4 leasing with rents significantly ahead of ERV across portfolio
body:
Time Period Quarter to 31 March 2026 (with full-year leasing and disposals for the year ended 31 March 2026)

  • Quarter leasing: 28 new leases/renewals signed.
  • Quarter rent contracted: £24.4m annual rent (company share: £23.8m).
  • Quarter market lettings vs ERV: on average 15.8% ahead of March 2025 ERV.
  • Fully Managed (quarter): 20 leases signed.
  • Fully Managed (quarter) rent roll added: £14.6m.
  • Fully Managed (quarter) average rent: £233 per sq ft.
  • Fully Managed (quarter) vs ERV: 8.6% ahead of March 2025 ERV.
  • Fitted (quarter): five leases signed.
  • Fitted (quarter) rent roll added: £1.6m.
  • Fitted (quarter) vs ERV: 7.2% ahead of March 2025 ERV.
  • Significant pre-let (quarter): 52,300 sq ft to Quantexa at The Delft, SE1 (formerly Minerva House), “significantly ahead” of March 2025 ERV.
  • Quantexa lease terms: 10-year lease; floors include ground east, first east, and fifth–seventh floors.
  • The Delft delivery: 143,100 sq ft HQ workspace targeted spring 2027; includes Thames frontage and 6,000 sq ft communal roof terrace.
  • Rent reviews (quarter): seven settled (including KKR and Glencore at Hanover Square, W1).
  • Rent reviews uplift: settled 49% ahead of previous passing rent.
  • Rent reviews vs ERV: settled 11.1% ahead of valuers’ ERV at review date.
  • Full-year leasing: 88 new leases/renewals signed.
  • Full-year rent contracted: £70.9m annual rent (company share: £69.6m).
  • Full-year market lettings vs ERV: on average 10.3% ahead of March 2025 ERV.
  • Leasing pipeline: £6.4m of rent under offer.
  • Under-offer pricing vs ERV: market lettings 7.9% ahead of March 2025 ERV.
  • Fully Managed demand: reported as “strong” in prime locations.
  • SIX St Andrew Street (EC4): final remaining office space let; third and fourth floors totalling 11,680 sq ft let to an “AI-led company”.
  • SIX St Andrew Street (EC4): now fully occupied; annual rent £8.8m.
  • SIX St Andrew Street (EC4): average rent £200 per sq ft.
  • SIX St Andrew Street (EC4): 6.2% ahead of ERV; 6.2% yield on cost.
  • Largest retail letting (quarter): Kent House, W1; 14,800 sq ft regear to Reiss Limited.
  • Reiss lease terms: new 10-year lease with break at five years; described as a customer of more than 25 years.
  • Development completion: 2 Aldermanbury Square completed in March; 321,650 sq ft of premium HQ workspace.
  • 2 Aldermanbury Square: stated as GPE’s “largest office development to date”; “delivered on time and on budget”.
  • 2 Aldermanbury Square: 100% pre-let; Clifford Chance (pre-let all offices) has taken occupation to begin fit-out.
  • Evelyn Estate agreement: new headlease + development agreement to refurbish 7/15 Gresse Street and 12/13 Rathbone Place (W1).
  • Evelyn Estate headlease: term extended by 20 years, pushing expiry to 2148.
  • Evelyn Estate scope: consent to convert to Fully Managed; will provide 42,800 sq ft across five floors.
  • Flex footprint: “Flex offer” totals 654,000 sq ft.
  • Rebrand: the Gresse/Rathbone buildings to be rebranded “The Howlett”.
  • Disposal: wells&more, W1 sold to Feldberg Capital (on behalf of Fastighets AB Balder) for £172m headline price.
  • wells&more pricing: 5.0% net initial yield; £1,483 per sq ft.
  • wells&more vs book: marginally ahead of September 2025 book value; around 5% ahead of March 2025 book value.
  • Disposal: 103/113 Regent Street, W1 (53-year short leasehold interest) sold for £52m headline price.
  • 103/113 Regent Street pricing: 7.2% net initial yield; £912 per sq ft.
  • 103/113 Regent Street vs book: “marginally behind” March 2025 book value.
  • Total sales (FY): £490m, stated as 2% ahead in aggregate of respective March 2025 book values.
  • Capital recycling: proceeds being reinvested into “new West End refurbishment opportunities at a significant discount” (no %/£ given).
  • Quote (CEO Toby Courtauld):

"Despite a volatile macroeconomic backdrop, this has been an excellent finish to the year. We signed £24.4 million of leases in the quarter and delivered a record £70.9 million of deals for the year, 10.3% ahead of ERV, reflecting the strength of demand for high quality, well located space and the momentum in our Fully Managed offer. Alongside this, we completed our largest office development to date at 2 Aldermanbury Square, which is 100% pre let, and progressed our capital recycling bringing sales to some £490 million, 2% ahead of March 2025 book value, reinvesting the proceeds into new West End refurbishment opportunities at a significant discount. Looking forward, we have further leasing under offer and a strong pipeline of new space in production, and so we start the new financial year with positive momentum."

summary: Ferrexpo's Q1 2026 production collapsed 72% due to Ukrainian infrastructure attacks and operational suspension
body:
Time Period 1Q 2026

  • Total production (1Q 2026): 593kt (592,751 tonnes in table), comprising 68kt Fe 67% premium concentrate and 525kt premium pellets.
  • Operational disruption: production activities “largely suspended” in Q1 due to nationwide attacks on Ukrainian electricity generation and transmission infrastructure.
  • Restart status: limited resumption at reduced capacity in late February 2026 after improvements in electricity availability and price.
  • Operating configuration: currently operating one of four pellet lines and selling to European customers.
  • Export logistics: continues to use its own fleet of rail wagons to export to customers in Eastern and Central Europe.
  • Safety metric: rolling 12-month LTIFR improved to 0.17 (vs historic five-year trailing average 0.44).
  • Safety record: zero workplace fatalities for more than five years.
  • Cost / liquidity actions (as per 1 April 2026 trading update, reiterated here): reduced working time, procurement cuts, continued suspension of all non-essential CapEx, overheads, and CSR spending.
  • Funding: actively exploring and progressing potential funding options, “which could include an equity capital raise.”
  • Funding risk statement: if VAT refunds withholding continues and funding issues aren’t resolved in time, “this could give rise to material negative consequences for the Group.”
  • Total commercial production vs 4Q 2025: 1,073,871 → 592,751 tonnes (-45%).
  • Total commercial production vs 1Q 2025: 2,125,467 → 592,751 tonnes (-72%).
  • Total pellet production vs 4Q 2025: 412,867 → 524,926 tonnes (+27%).
  • Total pellet production vs 1Q 2025: 1,347,749 → 524,926 tonnes (-61%).
  • DR pellets (FDP, 67% Fe): 0 (vs 81,787 tonnes in 1Q 2025; -100%).
  • Ferrexpo premium pellets (65% Fe) vs 1Q 2025: 1,105,049 → 524,926 tonnes (-52%).
  • Commercial concentrate (67% Fe) vs 4Q 2025: 661,004 → 67,825 tonnes (-90%).
  • Commercial concentrate (67% Fe) vs 1Q 2025: 777,718 → 67,825 tonnes (-91%).
  • Quote (Interim Executive Chair Lucio Genovese):

"The lower production numbers in the first quarter, at almost half of what we achieved in the final three months of 2025, reflect focused Russian attacks on Ukraine's power and energy infrastructure late last year and the resultant impact on our ability to operate sustainably. By January, given the supply of electricity could not be assured on a sustainable basis in the volumes required, we were forced to make the difficult decision to temporarily suspend operations and place a part of the workforce on furlough. Fortunately in late February we saw sufficient improvements in the availability and price of electricity to restart limited production at FPM on one pellet line. One pellet line remains in operation, and the Group continues to use its own fleet of rail wagons to export to customers in Eastern and Central Europe. We are grateful for the hard work across the country that has enabled repairs to be completed quickly. I am also grateful to colleagues who have worked tirelessly to restart the operations of one pellet line. This demonstrates, once again, our ability to be flexible and agile in a very challenging environment. Going forward, our focus remains on managing working capital and costs throughout this challenging operating environment."

summary: Hunting maintains full-year EBITDA guidance despite Q1 cash outflow and strong order book growth
body:
Time Period Q1 2026 (quarter trading update; order book as at 14 April 2026)

  • CEO Jim Johnson said: "Hunting has delivered a solid Q1 performance, and we are maintaining our full-year EBITDA guidance of $145-$155 million given the strong outlook for our end markets.”
  • CEO Jim Johnson said: "We remain vigilant regarding Middle East volatility, with our people remaining safe and our facilities fully operational.”
  • CEO Jim Johnson said: "We are seeing excellent order book momentum across South America and the US onshore market. By restructuring our global operations and continuing our share buyback programme, we are positioning Hunting for robust, long-term growth and enhanced shareholder returns."
  • Q1 trading in line with expectations; subsea contract milestones are weighted to Q2, impacting phasing.
  • Full-year EBITDA guidance maintained at $145–$155m; earnings expected 40:60 H1:H2 (second-half weighted).
  • Q1 2026 EBITDA $23.2m; EBITDA margin 10%.
  • Perforating Systems: North America ahead of expectations, attributed to “higher quality sales and production efficiencies” improving trading.
  • Working capital build (to satisfy committed orders) drove net cash outflow; combined with buybacks, quarter-end cash/(borrowings) $8.3m vs $62.9m at 31 Dec 2025 (down $54.6m / -86.8%).
  • Sales order book (as at 14 April 2026) c.$428.8m vs $365.3m at 31 March 2026 (+17.4%) and $358.0m at 31 Dec 2025 (+19.8%).
  • OCTG tender pipeline remains c.$1.0bn; Middle East tender issuance has slipped since March 2026, but management expects publication in Q2 2026.
  • Subsea order book building post year-end; includes $63.5m titanium stress joint orders for Guyana (announced 7 April 2026).
  • Increased momentum reported in Spring, Stafford and Flexible Engineered Solutions as offshore/subsea projects execute.
  • Non-energy order book (Advanced Manufacturing) at 31 March 2026: $95.6m vs $98.6m at 31 Dec 2025 (-3.0%).
  • Non-oil & gas growth push: targeting high-spec industrial programmes with repeat demand; cited customers include Teledyne Brown Engineering, Solar Turbines, Pratt & Whitney, Sikorsky, General Electric.
  • Non-energy programmes described as multi-year production schedules, with visibility in some cases “through 2028 and beyond.”
  • Middle East: Dubai and Saudi operations/personnel safe and generally unaffected; management expects minimal impact on current-year profitability (conditional on conflict duration).
  • Despite higher commodity prices, US/international rig count not yet increasing as operators adopt “wait and see”; Hunting positioned to benefit if activity rises.
  • EMEA restructuring: Fordoun site closure on track for June 2026.
  • $15m cost reduction programme accelerating; majority of savings expected by early 2028.
  • Shared service functions (Europe & North America) expected fully operational by mid-2026, aimed at further cost/operating efficiency and lower SG&A.
  • Org change: EMEA + Asia Pacific to combine into a single “International” segment from 1 Jan 2027, to support further cost efficiencies.
  • M&A: continuing to assess bolt-on acquisitions; Subsea highlighted as a focus area.
  • Next scheduled update: H1 2026 Trading Update due Wednesday 15 July 2026.

summary: Hollywood Bowl Group reports 9.5% revenue growth and opens new centres across UK and Canada
body:
Time Period Six months ended 31 March 2026 (H1 FY2026)

  • Group revenue £141.5m, up 9.5% vs H1 FY2025 (implies prior period c. £129.2m).
  • UK total revenue £118.4m, up 9.4% vs H1 FY2025 (implies prior period c. £108.2m).
  • Canada revenue CAD 42.9m (£23.2m), up 12.8% on a constant currency basis.
  • Group like-for-like (LFL) revenue +1.9%.
  • UK LFL +2.6%.
  • Canada LFL +0.5% (constant currency).
  • Cost/control framing: continued “disciplined cost management”; business has high gross margins and is positioned as insulated from inflation pressures.
  • Energy hedging: 76% of total electricity needs hedged until end of FY2029, including 12% from on-site solar.
  • Estate expansion: opened Edmonton (Canada) in H1; stated to be trading well.
  • Current estate: 77 UK centres and 16 Canadian centres (total 93).
  • Openings expected in H2: two new UK centres and one Canadian centre.
  • Pipeline: management highlights a strong new centre pipeline for FY2027 and beyond.
  • Capex: £8.6m invested in expansion, refurbishments and enhancements.
  • Balance sheet: net cash £26.0m at 31 March 2026; £25m revolving credit facility undrawn.
  • Outlook statement: Group remains confident for FY2026 and beyond.
  • CEO Stephen Burns said: "The benefits of the investments made throughout the UK and Canadian estate, combined with proactive demand generation initiatives and disciplined cost management, are reflected in our strong H1 performance. Demand for high-quality, family leisure activities that offer great value for money also remains resilient in both territories, and our cash generative business model allows us to invest where we see opportunities and deliver profitable growth."

Hunting PLC (duplicate / repeat of the earlier Hunting Q1 2026 trading update text) Time Period Q1 2026 (quarter trading update; order book as at 14 April 2026)

  • Repeats the same core disclosures as the earlier Hunting update: EBITDA guidance $145–$155m maintained; Q1 EBITDA $23.2m (10% margin); cash/(borrowings) $8.3m vs $62.9m at 31 Dec 2025; order book $428.8m as at 14 April 2026; OCTG pipeline c.$1.0bn; $63.5m Guyana titanium stress joint order; Fordoun closure June 2026; $15m cost reduction programme; EMEA + Asia Pacific combining into “International” from 1 Jan 2027; bolt-on M&A focus on Subsea.
  • Repeats the CEO quote in full: "Hunting has delivered a solid Q1 performance, and we are maintaining our full-year EBITDA guidance of $145-$155 million given the strong outlook for our end markets… We remain vigilant regarding Middle East volatility… We are seeing excellent order book momentum across South America and the US onshore market…"

summary: Standard Life to acquire Aegon UK for £2.0bn, creating UK's largest retirement business
body:
Time Period Transaction announcement (metrics referenced mainly FY2025; completion expected around end-2026)

  • Announced agreement to acquire Aegon UK plc (UK insurance & pensions ops of Aegon Europe Holding B.V.) for total consideration of £2.0bn.
  • Funding mix: debt + cash + new shares; Standard Life will issue new ordinary shares to Aegon representing ~15.3% of enlarged share capital on completion.
  • Cash component: £750m cash consideration, financed via £650m debt issuance prior to completion plus cash resources.
  • Equity component: 181.1m newly issued Standard Life shares to Aegon at completion; priced using 30-day VWAP of 690p (as at time of binding offer).
  • Aegon becomes a 15.3% shareholder; lock-up ends at earlier of 18 months post-completion or Aegon ultimate holding company re-domiciliation to the US (expected 1 January 2028).
  • Relationship agreement: Aegon entitled to appoint a non-executive director; also underpins a strategic asset management partnership.
  • Transaction expected to complete around end 2026, subject to regulatory conditions.
  • Positioning claim: combined business described as the UK’s largest retirement savings and income business, with ~16m customers and c.£480bn AUA.
  • Market ranking claims: combined group to become #2 in UK Workplace and #2 in UK Retail (by the measures referenced in the release).
  • Valuation: 0.83x Price/UT1, based on Aegon UK UT1 £2.4bn (FY2025).
  • Synergies / value: total net synergy value £0.8bn, comprising:

£110m run-rate pre-tax cost synergies (recurring). c.£340m one-off capital synergies.

  • Delivery timing on cost synergies: over half by end-2029, remainder by end-2031.
  • One-offs: ~£0.3bn post-tax integration costs plus £0.1bn additional post-tax separation costs.
  • Cash impact: expected to increase Operating Cash Generation by £160m per annum; also cites £0.4bn incremental excess cash over 5 years after financing and one-off costs.
  • Earnings impact: expected to add ~£190m IFRS adjusted operating profit per annum and be mid-single digit accretive to adjusted operating EPS by 2029.
  • Capital/leverage: funding consistent with existing Solvency II leverage target ~30%; states intention to maintain solvency coverage at the upper end of range.
  • On completion: anticipated single-digit uplift to Shareholder Capital Coverage Ratio (SCCR) and £1.25bn increase in IFRS shareholders’ equity.
  • Dividend policy: said to further underpin progressive and sustainable dividend on a per-share basis.
  • Strategic mix shift: operating profit contribution from capital-light fee-based growth earnings increases from 47% (FY25 standalone) to 57% for enlarged group (pro-forma post synergy realisation) (+10pp).
  • Workplace detail: adds £74bn AUA and 2.1m customers to Standard Life’s existing £71bn workplace offering; cites £18bn pro-forma gross annual flows (year ended 31 Dec 2025).
  • Retail detail: adds £86bn AUA and 1.8m customers; cites £12bn pro-forma gross annual flows (year ended 31 Dec 2025).
  • Group-wide uplift: adds ~£160bn AUA and 3.8m customers to create ~£480bn AUA / 16m customers.
  • Annuities capital allocation unchanged: commitment to allocate £200m capital per annum to annuities remains; continues exploring capital-light participation in pension risk transfer.
  • Support noted from existing strategic shareholders MS&AD Insurance Group Holdings, Inc and Aberdeen Group Plc (stated supportive and committed to shareholdings).
  • Standard Life CEO Andy Briggs said: "Our agreement to acquire Aegon UK significantly accelerates our vision to be the UK's leading retirement savings and income business. We will be in an even stronger position to meet the evolving needs of our 16 million customers with enhanced digital, advice and distribution capabilities across Workplace and Retail, strengthening our standing in one of the world's most attractive markets. Furthermore, the transaction accelerates our shift to capital-light whilst strengthening our cash, capital and earnings position to create increased value for shareholders.

summary: Aegon CEO announces Standard Life as the right owner for Aegon UK operations
body: • Aegon CEO Lard Friese said: "Standard Life is the right owner for Aegon UK: we share the same values and a strong commitment to customers, and together the businesses will create the UK's largest retirement savings and income provider. The businesses are complementary and the combination offers an excellent outcome for Aegon UK's customers and colleagues. Aegon's shareholding will provide an opportunity to participate in the future success of the enlarged group."

summary: PPHE Hotel Group to release Q1 2026 trading update on 29 April 2026
body:
Time Period Three months ended 31 March 2026 (update scheduled for release)

  • Issued a notice that it will announce a trading update for the three months ended 31 March 2026 on Wednesday 29 April 2026.
  • No financial or trading metrics disclosed in this notice.

summary: Segro's joint venture priced €500m five-year bond at 3.875% with strong demand
body:
Time Period Bond pricing announcement (dated 15 April 2026)

  • SELP joint venture (SEGRO acting as venture adviser) priced a €500m senior unsecured bond issue.
  • Bond tenor: 5 years.
  • Pricing: 105 bps above euro mid-swaps, equating to an annual coupon of 3.875%.
  • Demand: order book described as more than 10x covered at peak.

summary: Fiona Muldoon appointed Non-Executive Director at Grainger; Barratt Redrow on track with guidance
body:
In compliance with UK Listing Rule 6.4.9R(2), Admiral announces that with effect from 15 April 2026, Fiona Muldoon, Independent Non-Executive Director of Admiral, has been appointed as an independent Non-Executive Director of Grainger plc. Upon appointment, Fiona will be a member of the Grainger plc Nomination, Remuneration, Audit and Risk, and Responsible Business Committees. From mid-June, Fiona will be appointed the Grainger plc Senior Independent Director and Chair of the Audit and Risk Committee.

Barratt Redrow PLC on Wednesday said it is being "mores selective" on land purchases given the Middle East crisis and the likely impact on mortgage rates and build cost inflation.

The Leicestershire, England-based housebuilder said it is on track for total home completions of between 17,200 and 17,800 for the financial year to June, in line with consensus of 17,416, and higher than 16,565 completions in the 52 weeks to June 29, 2025.

The FTSE 100 listing said it is also on course to deliver full-year adjusted pretax profit in line with current market consensus of GBP568 million, which would be up 16% from GBP488.3 million the year prior.

Year-end net cash is now expected to be between GBP550 million and GBP650 million, around GBP150 million ahead of previous guidance, reflecting the timing of legacy building remediation payments and reduced land investment.

Barratt Redrow said it is being "mores selective" on land purchases given the Middle East crisis and the likely impact on mortgage rates and build cost inflation.

It now anticipates total land approvals for the financial year will be between 7,000 and 9,000 plots, below previous guidance range of between 10,000 and 12,000 plots, with land spend of between GBP700 million and GBP800m, lower than previous guidance range of between GBP800 million and GBP900 million.

"The ongoing conflict in the Middle East is contributing to increased economic uncertainty, including the potential for a more prolonged higher interest rate environment and renewed cost pressures. While we currently expect any direct impact on FY26 to be limited, visibility beyond the current financial year remains more uncertain," the firm added in a statement.

Shares in the company were up 1.9% at 263.10 pence each in London on Wednesday morning.

Barratt Redrow said it had a "solid" third quarter, which ran to March 29. Its net private reservation rate was 0.64 per active sales outlet per week, up on-year from 0.62.

It delivered 3,274 total home completions in the period down from 3,717 a year ago, bringing completions in the financial year to date to 10,718 homes versus 10,824, a year ago.

The lower level of completions in the period reflected a particularly strong comparable quarter last year ahead of the end of stamp duty relief.

Barratt Redrow maintained full-year build cost inflation guidance of 2% but stressed higher energy costs are likely to be reflected in increased building material costs in financial 2027.

In addition, the company confirmed its GBP100 million target in respect of cost synergies from the integration of Redrow.

Standard Life PLC on Wednesday said it has struck a GBP2.0 billion deal to acquire Aegon Europe Holding BV's UK insurance and pensions operations, in a cash and shares deal.

The Edinburgh-based insurance, savings and retirement products firm, formerly called Phoenix Group Holdings PLC, said Aegon will become a "new strategic shareholder and asset management partner" with a stake of just over 15%.

Standard Life will pay GBP750 million in cash. That portion will be funded by a GBP650 million debt issuance and existing cash resources. It will issue 181.1 million shares to Aegon.

The Aegon holding is subject to a lock up ending on the earlier of 18 months from completion or the completion of the redomiciliation of Aegon's holding company to the US, which is expected at the start of 2028.

"The relationship agreement with Aegon entitles Aegon to appoint a non-executive director to the board and underpins the strategic asset management partnership between Aegon and Standard Life," Standard Life said.

The company added that its existing strategic shareholders, MS&AD Insurance Group Holdings Inc and Aberdeen Group PLC, are supportive of the strategy and remain committed to their shareholdings.

"The potential for significant value creation of the enlarged group is attractive for them, and we look forward to continuing the development of our already close association with both," Standard Life added.

The company said the combination of the businesses accelerates its vision to be "the UK's leading retirement savings and income business" and the shift towards "capital-light, pensions and savings earnings".

Standard Life said the enlarged group will have 16 million customers and assets under administration of around GBP480 billion.

The deal delivers a total net synergy value of GBP800 million, including GBP100 million of run-rate pretax cost synergies and GBP340 million of one-off capital synergies.

Standard Life said the acquisition will lead to GBP160 million operating cash generation and GBP190 million adjusted operating profit, with a mid-single digit accretive to adjusted operating earnings per share by 2029.

The transaction is subject to regulatory conditions and is expected to complete around the end of 2026.

"Our agreement to acquire Aegon UK significantly accelerates our vision to be the UK's leading retirement savings and income business. We will be in an even stronger position to meet the evolving needs of our 16 million customers with enhanced digital, advice and distribution capabilities across Workplace and Retail, strengthening our standing in one of the world's most attractive markets," said Standard Life Chief Executive Officer Andy Briggs.

"Furthermore, the transaction accelerates our shift to capital-light whilst strengthening our cash, capital and earnings position to create increased value for shareholders."

Antofagasta PLC on Monday reported a decrease in quarterly copper production, but maintained annual guidance and left its cost targets unchanged, assuming fuel prices return to their January levels.

Shares in Antofagasta rose 3.3% to 4,004.00 pence on Wednesday morning in London, having more than doubled over the past year.

The London-based mining house targeting Chile estimated copper production of 143,000 tonnes in the first quarter of 2026, down 19% from 177,000 tonnes in the fourth quarter of 2025, and down 7.6% from 154,700t a year prior.

Antofagasta attributed this to "lower processing rates, and lower copper grades", but said its mine plan remained on track at the Los Pelambres and Centinela concentrators.

Copper sales decreased 32% on-quarter to 137,000t from 201,000t, and 20% on-year from 170,200t.

Gold production declined to 46,500 ounces from 66,300 ounces the preceding quarter, but were 8.4% ahead of the level reported a year earlier, reflecting higher gold grades, partially offset by lower ore processing rates at Los Pelambres and Centinela.

Molybdenum output edged down to 3,000t from 4,400t on-quarter and 3,100t on-year, through a combination of higher molybdenum recoveries and lower ore processing rates.

Cash costs before by-product credits jumped 17% on-year to 2.77 per pound, due to higher costs at the two concentrators. However, by-product credits more than doubled to USD1.69 per pound, strengthened by gold and molybdenum pricing, meaning net cash costs were 30% lower than the USD1.54 figure posted a year prior.

Antofagasta said its main projects were track in terms of timing and budget, as it reaffirmed full-year guidance.

The company continues to expect annual copper production ranging from 650,000t to 700,000t. In 2025, copper output had decreased 1.6% to 653,700t from 664,000t in 2024.

It also left cash cost targets unchanged, eyeing a range of USD2.30 to USD2.50 per tonne before by-product credits and USD1.15 to USD1.35 per tonne after credits.

This guidance assumes fuel prices return to January 2026 levels during the course of the second quarter, normalising from the spike prompted by conflict in the Middle East and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Antofagasta also reiterated annual capital expenditure guidance of USD3.4 billion.

In 2025, Antofagasta's revenue grew 30% on-year to USD8.62 billion, but lagged behind Peel Hunt expectations of USD8.68 billion. Operating profit from subsidiaries and share of total results from associates and joint ventures climbed 64% to USD3.43 billion, but was slightly below Peel Hunt's USD3.45 billion consensus estimate.

Antofagasta Chief Executive Ivan Arriagada commented on Wednesday: "As we move through the year, we expect copper production to increase quarter-on-quarter, supported by higher ore processing rates and improving grades at Los Pelambres, in line with the mine plan.

The CEO continued: "Pre-commissioning activities are now underway at the Centinela Second concentrator project, while progress across the Los Pelambres growth enabling projects continues to strengthen the operational platform for future production growth, as we look to increase our copper production by 30%.

"Against a backdrop of higher energy prices, we remain focused on safety, operational excellence, disciplined cost control and the timely execution of our growth and development projects, while maintaining resilient supply chains, and to date we have experienced no supply disruptions. The copper price remains constructive in 2026, and the medium-term fundamentals for copper are compelling, underpinned by structural demand drivers and constrained supply."

By Holly Munks, Alliance News reporter

ASOS PLC 15 April 2026

15 April 2026

summary: Natasja Laheij joins PostNL Supervisory Board; Therese Esperdy appointed Rentokil Chair
body:
In accordance with UK Listing Rule 6.4.6, the Company announces that Natasja Laheij, ASOS Chair, has been appointed to the Supervisory Board of PostNL N.V. ("PostNL"), a mail, parcel and e-commerce services provider listed on Euronext Amsterdam, with effect from 14 April 2026. Natasja will also serve as Chair of PostNL's Audit Committee.

Rentokil Initial PLC - Crawley, England-based pest control services - Appoints Therese Esperdy as its new chair, effective from September 1. Says she will initially join the board on July 1 as a non-executive director and chair designate. Esperdy is currently non-executive chair at Bristol, England-based tobacco products manufacturer Imperial Brands PLC, which announced her forthcoming retirement in late January. Imperial Brands said Esperdy, who assumed the role in 2020 and served on its board since 2016, would step down on December 1, when Informa PLC Chair John Rishton will succeed her after joining as chair designate on July 13. Esperdy is also senior independent director designate at Smith & Nephew plc and a Moody's Corp non-executive director. Rentokil announced on March 5 that outgoing Chair Richard Solomons intended to step down.

"It has been an immense privilege to serve as chair of Rentokil Initial over what has been a period of growth and transformation for the business," Solomons says. "With the successful appointment of Mike Duffy as CEO, and a clear strategy developed for North American growth, 2026 is the right time for me to step down and ensure a smooth handover to Therese. I have great confidence in the future success of Rentokil Initial - it's a high-quality business, full of exceptional people, operating in highly resilient growth markets."

Hollywood Bowl Group PLC on Tuesday announced "strong" revenue growth for the six months ended March 31, and remains confident in its outlook for the financial year 2026.

The ten-pin bowling centre operator saw reported revenue growth to GBP141.5 million, up 9.5% from the first half of their financial year 2025, according to a trading update. Like-for-like revenue growth was at 1.9%

UK revenue grew 9.4% to GBP118.4 million, or a 2.6% like-for-like growth.

Revenue in Canada was up 13% to CAD42.9 million, or 0.5% in like-for-like terms, when reviewing in Canadian dollars to allow for the disaggregation of foreign currency effect.

The Hemel Hempstead-based company noted its high gross margins, which make it "well-insulated against inflationary pressures," as well as a net cash position of GBP26.0 million with an undrawn GBP25 million revolving credit facility.

Stephen Burns, Chief Executive Officer, said: "The benefits of the investments made throughout the UK and Canadian estate, combined with proactive demand generation initiatives and disciplined cost management, are reflected in our strong first-half performance. Demand for high-quality, family leisure activities that offer great value for money also remains resilient in both territories, and our cash generative business model allows us to invest where we see opportunities and deliver profitable growth."

Hollywood Bowl also gave notice that it will report its interim results on May 27.

Great Portland Estates PLC on Wednesday hailed a strong fourth quarter as the end of "a successful year", in a trading statement ahead of its annual results.

The London-based office landlord will publish full-year earnings on May 21.

Great Portland shares rose 0.8% to 319.80 pence on Wednesday morning in London.

The company signed 28 leases and renewals in the three months ended in March, representing a share of GBP23.8 million in annual rent for Great Portland, which estimated that its market lettings were around 16% ahead of estimated rental value in March 2025.

In particular, the company pointed to a pre-letting of the Delft, formerly Minerva house, to data analytics and software firm Quantexa, which has leased the space for ten years. It has also let the last office space at its SIX St Andrew Street property to an artificial intelligence company, with the fully let building expected to generate GBP8.8 million in annual rent, which Great Portland said was 6.2% ahead of ERV and reflected a 6.2% yield on cost.

It noted two property disposals in the fourth quarter, with headline values of GBP172 million and GBP52 million respectively, bringing the total value of disposals in financial 2026 to GBP490 million. Great Portland said this was around 2% ahead of corresponding book values in March 2025.

It also settled seven rent reviews in the fourth quarter, in total 49% above the previous passing rent. This included leases with private equity firm Kohlberg, Kravis & Co LLP and mining house Glencore PLC.

In financial 2026 as a whole, Great Portland signed 88 leases and renewals representing a GBP69.6 million share of annual rent, with market lettings 10% higher than ERV a year prior.

Approximately GBP6.4 million in rent is currently under offer, Great Portland added.

Chief Executive Toby Courtauld commented: "Despite a volatile macroeconomic backdrop, this has been an excellent finish to the year...reflecting the strength of demand for high quality, well located space and the momentum in our fully managed offer.

The CEO added: "Looking forward, we have further leasing under offer and a strong pipeline of new space in production, and so we start the new financial year with positive momentum."

company: Schroder European Real Estate Investment Trust Ltd
summary: Portfolio declined 0.7% to €192.6m as lease gains offset tenant departures and defaults
body:
Time Period Quarter ended 31 March 2026 (portfolio valuation update)

  • Property portfolio independently valued at €192.6m (31 December 2025: €194.0m) — -€1.4m / -0.7% over the quarter.
  • Valuation outcome described as supported by “successful lease extensions” at Rumilly and Stuttgart, offset by “tenant-related setbacks” (notably Alkmaar and Cannes).
  • Rumilly logistics: valuation +€2.0m / +19% following completion of an “income accretive 10-year lease extension (break after seven)” with the sole tenant.
  • Stuttgart office: valuation +€1.1m / +6% driven primarily by a new 10-year annually indexed lease with the largest tenant (State of Baden-Württemberg) at +18% vs previous passing rent.
  • Alkmaar industrial: valuation -€3.2m / -31% after the sole tenant ceased operations and did not fulfil long-term lease obligations due to financial difficulties.
  • Cannes car showroom: valuation -€0.9m / -13% due to shortening lease terms and capex assumptions, after the sole tenant issued notice to depart in September 2026.
  • Apeldoorn mixed-use data centre: prior valuations already anticipated KPN’s departure at end-2026; this quarter’s -€0.7m / -6% decline attributed “solely to the shortening of the lease term”.

summary: BP expects volatile 1Q26 results from Middle East tensions and delayed price realizations across operations
body:
Time Period First quarter 2026 trading statement (outlook vs 4Q 2025)

  • Statement provides bp’s current estimates/expectations for 1Q26 (not a full estimate of results); results scheduled for 28 April 2026.
  • Highlights heightened volatility late in 1Q26 tied to “the ongoing situation in the Middle East” and market conditions — expected to impact results (including trading and working capital), increase dislocation between marker vs realised prices, and increase price lag impacts.
  • Reported upstream production expected broadly flat vs 4Q25 (2,344 mboe/d).
  • Gas & low carbon energy production expected slightly higher vs 4Q25 (788 mboe/d).
  • Oil production & operations production expected slightly lower vs 4Q25 (1,555 mboe/d), including price impacts on PSA and TSC entitlement volumes.
  • Gas & low carbon energy: realizations expected broadly flat vs 4Q25 (including price lags and changes in non-Henry Hub markers); gas marketing & trading expected average (4Q25: average).
  • Oil production & operations: realizations expected to impact vs prior quarter by +$0.1bn to +$0.2bn, reflecting “significant” price lag effects (noted particularly in the Gulf of America (one-month lag) and UAE (two-month lag)).
  • Oil production & operations: cash costs +$0.1bn vs prior quarter; DD&A broadly flat vs 4Q25 ($2.0bn), though with a higher unit charge.
  • Customers & products: customers results expected to reflect seasonally lower volumes and lower retail fuels margins, “more than offset” by stronger midstream.
  • Customers & products: products results expected to benefit from stronger realised refining margins of +$0.1bn to +$0.2bn and lower turnaround activity impact; oil trading expected to be exceptional (4Q25: weak).
  • Group underlying effective tax rate expected ~35% for 1Q26 (stated as reflecting higher results in products).
  • Organic capital expenditure expected broadly flat vs 4Q25 ($3.5bn).
  • Net debt expected $25bn–$27bn vs 4Q25 $22.2bn — increase of +$2.8bn to +$4.8bn (+12.6% to +21.6%), driven primarily by a working capital build of $4bn–$7bn (largely due to the price environment).
  • Trading conditions (marker indicators): Brent $81.13/bbl vs $63.73/bbl in 4Q25 (+$17.40 / +27.3%).
  • Trading conditions (marker indicators): Henry Hub $5.05/mmBtu vs $3.55/mmBtu in 4Q25 (+$1.50 / +42.3%).
  • Trading conditions (marker indicators): bp RIM $16.9/bbl vs $15.2/bbl in 4Q25 (+$1.7 / +11.2%).
  • Rules of thumb for full-year 2026 (pre-tax RC operating profit sensitivity): Brent ±$1/bbl → $340m; Henry Hub ±$0.10/mmBtu → $40m; RIM ±$1/bbl → $550m.
  • Appendix reminders (full-year guidance previously given, still applicable unless stated): capex $13.0bn–$13.5bn (weighted to 1H); divestment/other proceeds $9bn–$10bn (incl ~$6bn from announced Castrol transaction, weighted to 2H); Gulf of America oil settlement payments ~$1.6bn pre-tax (incl $0.4bn in 1Q and $1.1bn in 2Q); OB&C around $1bn charge.

summary: MAB acquires HomeOwners Alliance for £1.4m to expand earlier in homebuying journey
body:
Time Period N/A (acquisition announcement)

  • Acquired 100% of HomeOwners Alliance Ltd (HOA) from its founders and Smoove Limited.
  • Total consideration: £1.4m + surplus distributable reserves.
  • Payment structure: 50% on completion, remainder payable over a two-year period.
  • HOA described as a UK consumer platform attracting “millions of web visits annually”, providing guidance, tools and services to homeowners and prospective buyers.
  • Strategic rationale: expand presence across the wider home-moving ecosystem, enabling earlier engagement in the homebuying journey and extending beyond the point of mortgage advice.
  • Positions MAB ahead of potential changes arising from the Government’s ongoing home-selling and buying consultation.
  • Expected integration benefits: expand reach earlier in the process; increase lead volumes and extend services (including protection); strengthen links between mortgage advice and complementary home-moving services.
  • Ben Thompson (Director of Home Moving Strategy) quote: “HomeOwners Alliance has built a strong reputation as a trusted source of information for homeowners and prospective buyers. This acquisition strengthens our ability to engage with customers earlier in their homebuying journey and supports our strategy to broaden MAB's role across the wider home-moving process. We're pleased to welcome HomeOwners Alliance to the Group and look forward to supporting its continued growth.”
  • Paula Higgins (Founder & CEO, HOA) quote: “HomeOwners Alliance was created to help people navigate one of the biggest financial decisions of their lives. By joining Mortgage Advice Bureau, we will benefit from additional resources and reach, allowing us to expand the guidance, tools, and research we provide. We will continue to operate independently, retaining a strong voice for consumers, and are excited about our growth plans under MAB's ownership.”

summary: Fiona Muldoon appointed non-executive director at Grainger plc with senior roles
body:
Time Period N/A (directorate update; future effective dates)

  • Fiona Muldoon (independent non-executive director at Beazley) appointed independent non-executive director of Grainger plc effective 15 April 2026.
  • Fiona Muldoon to become Senior Independent Director and Chair of the Audit and Risk Committee at Grainger plc effective 18 June 2026.
  • At Grainger plc, she will also be a member of the Remuneration, Nominations and Responsible Business Committees.

summary: Fiona Muldoon appointed to Grainger Board as Non-Executive Director and future Senior Independent Director
body:
Time Period Announcement dated 14 April 2026 (appointments effective 15 April 2026; Board retirement 18 June 2026)

  • Fiona Muldoon appointed to the Board as Non-Executive Director (NED) with effect from 15 April 2026.
  • Muldoon will succeed Justin Read as Senior Independent Director and Chair of the Audit & Risk Committee when he retires from the Board after nine years on 18 June 2026.
  • In addition to joining the Audit & Risk Committee, Muldoon will also join the Board’s Remuneration, Nominations and Responsible Business Committees.
  • Muldoon background: Chartered Accountant; Fellow of the Institute of Chartered Accountants in Ireland; experience cited across capital markets, risk, governance and sustainability.
  • Current roles: Board member at Beazley plc (Risk Committee Chair, Audit Committee member, “Employee voice on the Board”) and Admiral Group plc (Audit Committee Chair, Risk Committee member).
  • Prior roles highlighted: CEO of FBD Holdings plc (2015–2020); Director of Credit Institutions and Insurance Supervision at the Central Bank of Ireland; 17 years with XL Group; NED at Bank of Ireland (served on all committees; chaired Sustainability Committee).
  • Simon Fraser (Chair) said:

“We are very pleased to welcome Fiona Muldoon to the Board as Chair of our Audit & Risk Committee and Senior Independent Director, bringing a significant amount of experience and expertise in finance and consumer facing financial services. “We'd like to extend our sincere thanks to Justin Read for his unwavering commitment and support to the business over his tenure on the Board, as Chair of Audit & Risk Committee and as Senior Independent Director, over which time we have seen tremendous growth and transformation.”

  • Fiona Muldoon said:

“I am delighted to join Grainger's Board and I look forward to taking on the roles of Senior Independent Director and Chair of the Audit & Risk Committee. Grainger is a brilliant business with strong future prospects to deliver value for shareholders and renters alike. I look forward to working with my fellow Board members as well as the wider executive team.”

  • Board determination: Muldoon is independent under the UK Corporate Governance Code (2024) and has “recent and relevant financial experience”; no further disclosure required under FCA Listing Rules 6.4.8R.

summary: Steve Mogford appointed Intertek chairman following May 2026 AGM
body:
Time Period Announcement dated 14 April 2026 (Intertek AGM 20 May 2026)

  • Steve Mogford (Costain Non-Executive Director) to be appointed Chairman of Intertek Group plc following Intertek’s AGM on 20 May 2026.
  • Mogford is Chair of Costain’s Sustainability Committee and a member of all Costain Board Committees.

Time Period Announcement dated 14 April 2026 (appointments effective 15 April 2026; Board retirement 18 June 2026)

  • Fiona Muldoon appointed to the Board as Non-Executive Director (NED) with effect from 15 April 2026.
  • Muldoon will succeed Justin Read as Senior Independent Director and Chair of the Audit & Risk Committee when he retires from the Board after nine years on 18 June 2026.
  • In addition to joining the Audit & Risk Committee, Muldoon will also join the Board’s Remuneration, Nominations and Responsible Business Committees.
  • Muldoon background: Chartered Accountant; Fellow of the Institute of Chartered Accountants in Ireland; experience cited across capital markets, risk, governance and sustainability.
  • Current roles: Board member at Beazley plc (Risk Committee Chair, Audit Committee member, “Employee voice on the Board”) and Admiral Group plc (Audit Committee Chair, Risk Committee member).
  • Prior roles highlighted: CEO of FBD Holdings plc (2015–2020); Director of Credit Institutions and Insurance Supervision at the Central Bank of Ireland; 17 years with XL Group; NED at Bank of Ireland (served on all committees; chaired Sustainability Committee).
  • Simon Fraser (Chair) said:

“We are very pleased to welcome Fiona Muldoon to the Board as Chair of our Audit & Risk Committee and Senior Independent Director, bringing a significant amount of experience and expertise in finance and consumer facing financial services. “We'd like to extend our sincere thanks to Justin Read for his unwavering commitment and support to the business over his tenure on the Board, as Chair of Audit & Risk Committee and as Senior Independent Director, over which time we have seen tremendous growth and transformation.”

  • Fiona Muldoon said:

“I am delighted to join Grainger's Board and I look forward to taking on the roles of Senior Independent Director and Chair of the Audit & Risk Committee. Grainger is a brilliant business with strong future prospects to deliver value for shareholders and renters alike. I look forward to working with my fellow Board members as well as the wider executive team.”

  • Board determination: Muldoon is independent under the UK Corporate Governance Code (2024) and has “recent and relevant financial experience”; no further disclosure required under FCA Listing Rules 6.4.8R.

summary: Mortgage Advice Bureau acquired HomeOwners Alliance for £1.4m to expand home-moving services
body:
Time Period Announcement (acquisition)

  • Acquired London-based property advice platform HomeOwners Alliance Ltd (HOA) to engage customers earlier in the home-buying process.
  • Total consideration is GBP1.4m + distributable reserves.
  • Consideration structure: 50% on completion (~GBP0.7m) and 50% deferred over two years (~GBP0.7m).
  • Acquired 100% of the share capital from HOA’s founders and Smoove Ltd.
  • Rationale: strengthens positioning ahead of potential changes from the government’s home-selling and buying consultation.
  • Operational intent: integrate/enhance HOA’s digital platform to increase lead volumes and extend the range of services available.
  • Strategic intent: strengthen links between mortgage advice and complementary home-moving services.
  • Share move noted: shares were +1.7% at 585.0p (Tuesday morning).
  • Ben Thompson (Director of Home Moving Strategy) quote: “HomeOwners Alliance has built a strong reputation as a trusted source of information for homeowners and prospective buyers. This acquisition strengthens our ability to engage with customers earlier in their homebuying journey and supports our strategy to broaden MAB's role across the wider home-moving process. We're pleased to welcome HomeOwners Alliance to the Group and look forward to supporting its continued growth.”

summary: Morgan Stanley cuts UK housebuilder ratings citing geopolitical risks and weakening rate outlook
body:
Time Period Sector / market note (no financial period stated)

  • View: UK housebuilders face rising “macro risk” as interest-rate expectations pick up and the Middle East / Iran conflict shifts the backdrop “materially”.
  • Mechanism described: geopolitical risk → higher oil/energy prices → higher swap rates → reversal of expected Bank of England rate cuts → weaker housing demand.
  • Sector conclusion: a re-rate is unlikely without a clear improvement in the rate outlook, despite cheap valuations.
  • Morgan Stanley quote: “Valuation support alone has repeatedly failed to act as a catalyst in this cycle,” (analysts).
  • Barratt Redrow rating cut to Equal-weight (from Overweight).
  • Barratt Redrow price target cut to 300p from 430p (-30.2%).
  • Barratt Redrow share move noted: -0.2% to 259.9p (Tuesday afternoon).
  • Barratt Redrow framing: “scale and a multi-brand platform offer strategic flexibility,” but revenue synergies constrained in weak demand; performance increasingly macro-driven.
  • Berkeley Group rating cut to Underweight (from Equal-weight).
  • Berkeley price target cut to 2,990p from 3,840p (-22.1%).
  • Berkeley share move noted: -0.6% to 3,430.0p (Tuesday afternoon).
  • Berkeley rationale: has cut profit forecasts, paused land acquisition, and is prioritising balance sheet strength over growth amid weak London/South East demand; re-phasing “Berkeley 2035” delivery to 2030 referenced.
  • Persimmon price target cut to 1,390p from 1,610p (-13.7%) while retaining Overweight and described as housebuilding preference.
  • Persimmon share move noted: +0.3% to 1,144.0p (Tuesday afternoon).
  • Persimmon rationale: “cleanest” exposure to a volume-led recovery if rates ease; highlighted above-average margins/returns and limited London exposure.
  • Taylor Wimpey price target cut to 80p from 90p (-11.1%) and rated Underweight.
  • Taylor Wimpey share move noted: +0.1% to 85.14p (Tuesday afternoon).
  • Vistry price target cut to 380p from 510p (-25.5%) and rated Equal weight.
  • Vistry share move noted: +1.8% to 332.6p (Tuesday afternoon).

summary: CME Group launching options on Eris SOFR Swap futures in June 2026
body:
Time Period Announcement (product launch planned)

  • Will launch options on Eris SOFR Swap futures on 16 June 2026, pending regulatory review.
  • Options will be listed on 2-year, 5-year and 10-year Eris SOFR Swap futures (aimed at more sophisticated hedging / non-linear risk).
  • Stated use-case: help manage U.S. dollar interest rate risk, including non-linear risk in mortgage-backed portfolios.
  • “Since launching in October 2020, more than 10 million Eris SOFR Swap futures contracts have traded at CME Group.”
  • “In March 2026, Eris SOFR Swap futures reached an all-time open interest record of 707,000 contracts ($71B notional), including a single-day volume record of 299,513 contracts on March 10.”
  • Options will be eligible for margin offsets with other cleared interest rate futures/options at CME Group; products listed by / subject to the rules of CBOT.
  • Agha Mirza quote: “Our new options on Eris SOFR Swap futures will provide clients with additional flexibility in managing U.S. dollar interest rate risk…”
  • Michael Riddle quote: “Eris SOFR Swap options will help institutional investors manage risk with greater precision… By mirroring the structure of forward-premium OTC swaptions…”
  • Don Wilson quote: “CME Group's addition of options will enable more sophisticated risk management strategies while preserving the capital and liquidity advantages…”
  • Jeff Bauman quote: “Eris SOFR Swap options will enable clients to better manage the convexity of their portfolios, while providing a flexible, capital-efficient extension…”

summary: Florence Copper began production in Q1 2026 while Gibraltar significantly increased output
body:
Time Period Q1 2026

  • Florence Copper SX/EW plant commenced operations mid-February 2026; first copper cathodes harvested end-February 2026.
  • Florence Copper Q1 production: 1.5 million pounds of copper cathode produced.
  • Solutions flowing in the wellfield since late 2025; initial leaching/production described as in line with modelling/expectations.
  • Operational focus recently: balancing solution flow and grades from wellfield through to cathode production.
  • Stuart McDonald quote: “We are happy with the results from the first months of in-situ copper production at Florence… the focus is turning to the production ramp-up…”
  • Ramp-up / expansion: additional newly constructed wells being integrated; wellfield expansion continues with four drills operating and a fifth expected to commence shortly.
  • Gibraltar Mine Q1 production: 30.0 million pounds copper and 717 thousand pounds molybdenum.
  • Gibraltar YoY production change vs Q1 2025: copper +50%, molybdenum +113%.
  • Gibraltar: copper grades in line with life-of-mine average; recoveries improved to 83%.
  • Gibraltar Q1 copper sales: 27 million pounds, slightly lower than production due to shipment timing.
  • Gibraltar SX/EW plant produced 733 thousand pounds of copper cathode and operated continuously through winter months.
  • Market/cost commentary: average LME copper price in Q1 was +16% vs previous quarter.
  • Cost sensitivity: diesel prices at current levels would raise Gibraltar operating costs by approximately US$0.10–0.15/lb this year.
  • Input hedging: Florence has a fixed price contract for all sulphuric acid requirements in 2026, so expects no impact from recent inflation/supply-chain issues.
  • McDonald quote (outlook): “Overall, we continue to anticipate strong financial performance from steady Gibraltar production and growing production from Florence Copper.”

summary: Taseko Mines Q1 2026 production meets guidance with Florence SX/EW ramping
body:

  • Market reaction noted: shares closed +3.7% at 549.5p (London, Tuesday).
  • Recaps the same operational/production disclosures: Florence SX/EW start-up timing, 1.5m lbs Q1 cathode; Gibraltar 30.0m lbs copper and 717k lbs molybdenum; recoveries 83%; Q1 sales 27m lbs; diesel sensitivity US$0.10–0.15/lb; sulphuric acid fixed-price contract for 2026; and the CEO quotes on ramp-up/financial outlook.

summary: Next reportedly weighs acquisition of struggling British handbag brand Radley via potential pre-pack administration
body:
Time Period Announcement / media report (no financial period for Next; Radley FY figures disclosed)

  • Sky News-reported: Next is weighing a takeover of Radley (British handbag brand).
  • Structure discussed: potential deal would involve acquiring brand and IP assets (not necessarily the whole operating company).
  • Sale-process dynamic: industry sources suggested it might be implemented via a pre-pack administration, with outcome “some way” from being determined.
  • Competitive context: Next is one of the suitors alongside turnaround investor Gordon Brothers (per report).
  • Positioning: would be another small “bolt-on” acquisition for Next; article cites recent Russell & Bromley purchase and prior brand deals (Cath Kidston, Joules, Seraphine).
  • Radley FY to April 2025: reported loss of GBP2.2m.
  • Radley revenue: GBP65.8m, down from GBP72.0m (YoY -8.6%).
  • Radley geographic mix: the US ~15% of group revenues (per report).

company: Schroder European Real Estate Investment Trust Ltd
summary: Portfolio declined €1.4m to €192.6m as lease gains offset tenant departures and defaults
body:
Time Period Quarter ended 31 March 2026 (portfolio valuation update)

  • Property portfolio independently valued at €192.6m (31 December 2025: €194.0m) — -€1.4m / -0.7% over the quarter.
  • Valuation outcome described as supported by “successful lease extensions” at Rumilly and Stuttgart, offset by “tenant-related setbacks” (notably Alkmaar and Cannes).
  • Rumilly logistics: valuation +€2.0m / +19% following completion of an “income accretive 10-year lease extension (break after seven)” with the sole tenant.
  • Stuttgart office: valuation +€1.1m / +6% driven primarily by a new 10-year annually indexed lease with the largest tenant (State of Baden-Württemberg) at +18% vs previous passing rent.
  • Alkmaar industrial: valuation -€3.2m / -31% after the sole tenant ceased operations and did not fulfil long-term lease obligations due to financial difficulties.
  • Cannes car showroom: valuation -€0.9m / -13% due to shortening lease terms and capex assumptions, after the sole tenant issued notice to depart in September 2026.
  • Apeldoorn mixed-use data centre: prior valuations already anticipated KPN’s departure at end-2026; this quarter’s -€0.7m / -6% decline attributed “solely to the shortening of the lease term”.

summary: BP expects flat upstream production in Q1 2026 amid Middle East volatility and trading impacts
body:
Time Period First quarter 2026 trading statement (outlook vs 4Q 2025)

  • Statement provides bp’s current estimates/expectations for 1Q26 (not a full estimate of results); results scheduled for 28 April 2026.
  • Highlights heightened volatility late in 1Q26 tied to “the ongoing situation in the Middle East” and market conditions — expected to impact results (including trading and working capital), increase dislocation between marker vs realised prices, and increase price lag impacts.
  • Reported upstream production expected broadly flat vs 4Q25 (2,344 mboe/d).
  • Gas & low carbon energy production expected slightly higher vs 4Q25 (788 mboe/d).
  • Oil production & operations production expected slightly lower vs 4Q25 (1,555 mboe/d), including price impacts on PSA and TSC entitlement volumes.
  • Gas & low carbon energy: realizations expected broadly flat vs 4Q25 (including price lags and changes in non-Henry Hub markers); gas marketing & trading expected average (4Q25: average).
  • Oil production & operations: realizations expected to impact vs prior quarter by +$0.1bn to +$0.2bn, reflecting “significant” price lag effects (noted particularly in the Gulf of America (one-month lag) and UAE (two-month lag)).
  • Oil production & operations: cash costs +$0.1bn vs prior quarter; DD&A broadly flat vs 4Q25 ($2.0bn), though with a higher unit charge.
  • Customers & products: customers results expected to reflect seasonally lower volumes and lower retail fuels margins, “more than offset” by stronger midstream.
  • Customers & products: products results expected to benefit from stronger realised refining margins of +$0.1bn to +$0.2bn and lower turnaround activity impact; oil trading expected to be exceptional (4Q25: weak).
  • Group underlying effective tax rate expected ~35% for 1Q26 (stated as reflecting higher results in products).
  • Organic capital expenditure expected broadly flat vs 4Q25 ($3.5bn).
  • Net debt expected $25bn–$27bn vs 4Q25 $22.2bn — increase of +$2.8bn to +$4.8bn (+12.6% to +21.6%), driven primarily by a working capital build of $4bn–$7bn (largely due to the price environment).
  • Trading conditions (marker indicators): Brent $81.13/bbl vs $63.73/bbl in 4Q25 (+$17.40 / +27.3%).
  • Trading conditions (marker indicators): Henry Hub $5.05/mmBtu vs $3.55/mmBtu in 4Q25 (+$1.50 / +42.3%).
  • Trading conditions (marker indicators): bp RIM $16.9/bbl vs $15.2/bbl in 4Q25 (+$1.7 / +11.2%).
  • Rules of thumb for full-year 2026 (pre-tax RC operating profit sensitivity): Brent ±$1/bbl → $340m; Henry Hub ±$0.10/mmBtu → $40m; RIM ±$1/bbl → $550m.
  • Appendix reminders (full-year guidance previously given, still applicable unless stated): capex $13.0bn–$13.5bn (weighted to 1H); divestment/other proceeds $9bn–$10bn (incl ~$6bn from announced Castrol transaction, weighted to 2H); Gulf of America oil settlement payments ~$1.6bn pre-tax (incl $0.4bn in 1Q and $1.1bn in 2Q); OB&C around $1bn charge.

summary: MAB acquires HomeOwners Alliance for £1.4m to expand home-moving services
body:
Time Period N/A (acquisition announcement)

  • Acquired 100% of HomeOwners Alliance Ltd (HOA) from its founders and Smoove Limited.
  • Total consideration: £1.4m + surplus distributable reserves.
  • Payment structure: 50% on completion, remainder payable over a two-year period.
  • HOA described as a UK consumer platform attracting “millions of web visits annually”, providing guidance, tools and services to homeowners and prospective buyers.
  • Strategic rationale: expand presence across the wider home-moving ecosystem, enabling earlier engagement in the homebuying journey and extending beyond the point of mortgage advice.
  • Positions MAB ahead of potential changes arising from the Government’s ongoing home-selling and buying consultation.
  • Expected integration benefits: expand reach earlier in the process; increase lead volumes and extend services (including protection); strengthen links between mortgage advice and complementary home-moving services.
  • Ben Thompson (Director of Home Moving Strategy) quote: “HomeOwners Alliance has built a strong reputation as a trusted source of information for homeowners and prospective buyers. This acquisition strengthens our ability to engage with customers earlier in their homebuying journey and supports our strategy to broaden MAB's role across the wider home-moving process. We're pleased to welcome HomeOwners Alliance to the Group and look forward to supporting its continued growth.”
  • Paula Higgins (Founder & CEO, HOA) quote: “HomeOwners Alliance was created to help people navigate one of the biggest financial decisions of their lives. By joining Mortgage Advice Bureau, we will benefit from additional resources and reach, allowing us to expand the guidance, tools, and research we provide. We will continue to operate independently, retaining a strong voice for consumers, and are excited about our growth plans under MAB's ownership.”
